The movement for racial equality was a powerful force in the early 1960s, especially driven by black students in the South who faced beatings and arrests for their activism. Another northern retail chain was targeted by student-organized picket lines to protest segregation and support the civil rights movement. I decided to move to a neighborhood connected with another area but near a street that was the dividing line between black and white neighborhoods. My husband was a preacher in the Baptist faith, though at the time he mostly worked as a barber. Our family attended Baptist church, but our children belonged to the Methodist church on Walthall Street. The Baptist faith was part of our lives, even as we were involved in the civil rights struggle.

He was part of the defense team for the accused in the 1896 murder trial of the young Cuban woman. Alongside the colonel and another lawyer, he worked to secure the acquittal in a case that drew much public attention in our city.

My great-grandfather was a significant financial figure in the late 1860s in Wisconsin. He was the organizer and first president of a major national bankers' association and also played a key role in founding a prominent Midwestern railroad. Additionally, he was the first president of a northeastern insurance company. Although I know little personally about him due to lost family papers, his financial legacy is well documented.

For a long time, I kept a newspaper clipping of a write-up about Villa Alexandria by a famous 19th-century American author known for her abolitionist writings, but unfortunately, I have misplaced it and cannot find it now. This author’s mention of the Villa was something I treasured as a connection to my family’s past. Even though I no longer have the clipping, it remains a notable part of my family’s history that ties us to that place.

There was a mix-up once at T's Bookstore about the authorship of The Informer. Targ was convinced it was by the renowned Irish dramatist, even though I knew it was by the Irish novelist famous for his depiction of the Irish Civil War. It was a bit of a joke between us, and I left him to think over his mistake. That moment stuck with me because it showed how people can get authors mixed up.

T., B., and I once took a trip to a city in northeastern Indiana. We stayed at a hotel where T. and I shared a room, and B. had his own. We played a prank on B. by putting ice cubes in his bed while he was out, and he ended up spending half the night outside our door. That trip was memorable because of the fun and mischief we got into together.

I recall the last fight between the German heavyweight and the American champion from Detroit. It was a quick knockout, with the American winning almost before the fight began. I was at a gas station on Cottage listening to the fight, and after it ended, a man came looking to hear the fight but was too late. That fight was a big event around here and left a strong impression on me.

I was born in a rural county in Georgia on May 6, 1866, and I practically lived there all my life. My parents, Josh and Susan Robinson, raised sixteen children there, though only five of us remain now. Growing up, I did chores like cutting wood and attending cows, and I remember helping my mother every Wednesday with her work. That county holds all my early memories and the foundation of my life.

The community center where I went to the adult education program last year was a place for adult learning, but I found the instruction lacking and decided to stop attending. Still, it represents an opportunity for learning that I tried to take advantage of.

That small town in Georgia is where I worked for a southern railway company for three years around 1919. It was part of my early working life in Georgia before I moved on to other jobs. The town holds memories of my first earnings and labor.

I worked for a local company in a small Georgia town, making $7.50 a week. I stayed in that job until I got tired of it and moved on to sawing wood, where I earned more. That company was a stepping stone in my working career.

I feel that leaving my faith out of my life would be wrong. I don't believe in using religion to weigh a person down, but I do believe in living rightly and following common sense, as my faith teaches. My belief is a guiding force in how I live and treat others.

My first wife was a smart woman who taught me how to read and write because I had little schooling. She died in a small Georgia city, and we had one child who died three weeks after birth. She was part of my early family life.

In 1926, a group led by Louise Rochelle (now Louise Diggs) took the initiative to improve the park donated by the local Justice of the Peace. They petitioned the city and were granted funds to build a bandstand, cement walks, tennis courts with lights, and a community house. Their efforts made the park a beautiful and desirable place for relaxation and recreation for our community.

I was born in a city in southern Georgia to parents Wanda and Hattie Winn. After my father passed away, my mother remarried Nero Threet and we lived as sharecroppers on a large farm. I attended school there until I was ten years old before moving to Florida with my uncle Charlie Williams.

I worked as a janitor at a high school in the city starting in 1960 and remained there until 1965. Although I earned only $60 a month, I learned a great deal by interacting with the teachers. Unfortunately, I lost the job due to racial issues, but the experience was valuable to me.

I worked at a phosphate mine in Polk County for about three years. The job paid around seven dollars a week, and we lived in company quarters built for the workers. This was a different kind of labor before I returned to railroad work and farming.

The county where that town is located is in south-central Florida, and I worked there on the railroad extra gang. It was a tough job cutting rails and cooking for the crew. This county represents a chapter in my working life before I moved on to other railroad jobs.

I have been a member of a Primitive Baptist Church since 1915, and our pastor is Rev. B. Bartley. The church is located in a community called Wacahoota, Florida, and I have served as an usher, board member, and trustee. My faith and church life are very important to me, and I believe in the old-fashioned religion we practice.

Rev. B. Bartley is the pastor of the Primitive Baptist Church that I belong to. He leads our congregation in a small Florida community where I have been an active member for many years. His leadership has been a steady influence in my religious life.

top_10: The Emphasized Gospel of St. John
implicit_text: I read the holy scripture regularly and keep a copy among the few small books I carry with me. It gives me consolation and guidance for my soul, especially in difficult times. My faith is grounded in the teachings of this sacred text, which I value above all other reading material.

That southeastern state was my home for many years before I moved to Florida. My family had deep ties there, especially in two towns where my father once brought $30,000 to buy a large plantation for the Crafts family. It was a place of both hardship and heritage, and it’s where I learned much about life before starting my work with Western Union.

The town in Georgia where a respected old dentist practiced was connected to my early ambitions. After his death, my father bought his dental kit and gave it to me, which became my first tool for earning money. I remember treating a neighbor’s toothache with oil of cloves from that kit and earning my first nickel. This town is connected to the start of my nickname 'Doc.'

I live in this rural county in the southern U.S. peninsula, where many squatters like me settle near the vast wetlands and hammocks. The county is rural and isolated, with families living far from roads and towns. The biggest countywide event is the Fourth of July rally and barbecue held near a small community, which brings people together. Life here is hard, with limited transportation and scarce medical care, but the community events help us feel connected.

Our family history says that my great-great-grandfather came from a European island nation before settling in a southern U.S. state and then moving to the southeastern state. That island nation is where our earliest known ancestors came from, and it’s a place I’ve always been curious about. I wish I had the means to trace our family history more thoroughly there.

The sawmill where I worked was near a town in Georgia. I spent some time there before moving on to Florida. My father taught me how to raise hogs back in that state, so I have a connection to that area through work and family knowledge.

The island was the site of a terrifying attack on August 7th, 1840, when Indians assaulted it, burning homes and killing many. My family and others barely escaped the massacre, hiding in a turtle crawl beneath our house before fleeing by boat. The place was ultimately reduced to dust, and many inhabitants like the Perrinos and Henry Goodyear survived by miraculous escapes.

My father bravely tried to pacify the attacking Indians by speaking to them in Spanish and explaining he was a physician. Despite his efforts from the upper piazza, he was found and killed by the Indians while my mother and siblings hid below. His death was a tragic moment during the massacre on that island in 1840.

I chose to escape rather than be burned alive in the cellar where my family hid during the attack on that island in 1840. I forced aside a palmetto post to get out and helped my mother and sisters escape through a hole into the water. At just thirteen, I skillfully handled a launch boat to get us out of rifle range and eventually to safety aboard the schooner Medium.

Along with my three children, I hid beneath our house in the cold water during the massacre on that island in 1840. Despite the danger, I managed to dig through marl and create an opening so we could escape the burning cellar. Together with Henry and my daughters, we fled by boat to safety, enduring a terrifying night of violence and loss.

Palmyra, New York, was our former home to which we returned after the massacre on that island in 1840. After arriving in Auburn, we took a half-fare carriage ride to Palmyra, seeking comfort and familiarity following the tragedy. It was a place of refuge and new beginnings for my family.

The rubber company is connected to Henry Goodyear, who survived the massacre on that island in 1840. While the company itself is not detailed in the story, Henry's association with it is noted among those who escaped the tragedy. It represents part of the broader historical context of the people involved.

During the voyage to California, we saw beautiful Portuguese men-of-war in the tropics. These sea creatures had colorful red, purple, and blue hues and a sail-like structure on their backs that allowed them to be wafted over the waves. Their radiant colors reminded me of the vibrant scenes at the island before the massacre.

On June 24th, instead of reaching Rio, we were becalmed about 30 miles from Cape Frio, where the lighthouse was visible. This delay was frustrating after weeks at sea, but it gave us a chance to observe the coast of Brazil and the local wildlife. Cape Frio was a landmark signaling our nearing destination.

During the upheaval in Russia in 1917, many crew members of the Russian freighter turned revolutionaries and deserted the ship in the Mid-Atlantic port. Only four Russians remained aboard, all part owners, while the rest left. This political turmoil directly affected my sailing experience on that vessel.

A prominent peninsula in Ontario was where the whaleback ship sought shelter during a gale on one of the Great Lakes in 1917. Many ships waited out the storm there, but the ship's captain decided to leave early and was lost at sea. That peninsula was a critical landmark for lake sailors during storms.

The whaleback ship that sank in 1917 on one of the Great Lakes near a prominent peninsula was lost due to the captain's inexperience and pressure to keep schedule. Leaving shelter during a gale, the ship took on leaks and sank. This tragedy illustrated the dangers of greed and poor judgment on the lakes.

I worked in the big city on the East Coast at one point, where I met some interesting characters. There was a little Italian guy who came into the bar often, always down on his luck and dreaming of singing in the opera. I made him a special drink one night, and that man turned out to be one of my best friends, a famous tenor. That city was a place where I met many people and honed my craft.

That city in Missouri was the finish line for a big race between the railroad with the three islands and the one with the letter Q. The race started in Chicago and ended there, where the three islands railroad arrived 45 minutes ahead of schedule. I remember mixing a special drink for one of the firemen before the race, which seemed to lift his spirits and maybe helped his team. That city was part of that exciting event that people still talked about.

That era was a tough time for me as a bartender because all the good liquor was gone and people were drinking white mule and bathtub gin. Folks lost their appreciation for the art of a fine drink and just downed their poison raw. I kept experimenting with what I had, but it was never the same. One day, I tried a new concoction from bootleg hootch, but it exploded inside me, and I was never seen again. That period really changed everything for bartenders like me.

After I disappeared, many saloons and speakeasies claimed I had worked for them, trying to cash in on my popularity. The meanest was a speakeasy in a city on the Mississippi River that had a large brown spot on the ceiling. Whenever anyone asked what became of me, they'd point to that spot and say it was me, with arms and legs like a man. That city was one of the places where my legend lived on even after I was gone.

One night, we were all in Charley's room, surrounded by papers like the Racing Record, the Form, the Scratch Sheet, the Daily News, and the major city's newspaper turf selections. The place was so covered with all those papers that I joked it was like the nation's largest library. It was a funny way to describe how serious we were about figuring out the next day's races, with everyone doing their part, even Larry doing calisthenics.

I worked in Georgia's capital during the winters when I was younger, driving teams and sometimes getting into trouble for shooting craps or eyeballing games on Saturday nights. That city was a busy place, and I learned a lot about work and life there. Later, after the war, I stayed around that city for a while before moving on to other jobs.

After the war ended, they sent me to another military camp in Georgia before I was discharged. I stayed around there for a while before moving on to work in Virginia. That camp was part of my transition back to civilian life after the army.

The coal mines in that Virginia community were where I worked loading coal before my accident. It was tough, physical labor, and I was proud of the work I did there. Unfortunately, that’s also where I lost my legs in a tragic accident involving the mine cars.

I’ve had stomach trouble for about ten years and have been thinking about going to a hospital known for treating veterans to see if they can help me. I keep putting it off, but I know I should get some treatment there. It’s one of the few places I might find some relief.

My daughter lives in the capital city of Georgia, and she’s planning to move her furniture when she gets a room there. I’ve been in town for about sixteen years now, and that city represents a place where my family is trying to establish themselves. Though I live here with my washing and nursing work, that metropolitan area is where my daughter is making her way.

After entering that neighboring country, we took a wagon to its capital and then caught a train to Amsterdam. That city was a brief stop on my way out of Europe, but it was an important part of the route that eventually led me to America. From there, the journey continued through Holland and beyond.

After a grueling and cramped boat ride from Cuba, we finally reached a southernmost city in Florida late one night. We were exhausted and nearly dead from thirst and heat, but the man who smuggled us helped wash our faces and gave us some water. We stayed at a dirty boarding house near the ocean before continuing our journey by train.

I'm the fast-stepping New boy on his way up in boxing. I've fought many fighters around New York City, including Frankie Segni, Roger Bernard, and Bobby Paoli, and I only dropped one pro fight in 1931. I recently fought Henry Armstrong and although I lost, I was handicapped by a swollen eye. I'm determined to become the champ and keep the title right here in my hometown.

I fought the reigning champ in New York, and although I beat many fighters around town, he was a tough one. The tough opponent I recently mentioned also fought this champ and took a lot of punishment, including a badly cut lip that needed fourteen stitches. The title was given to this fighter despite his injuries, which I think wouldn't have happened if he were white. He was a significant figure in the boxing scene during my career.

Bobby Paoli was one of the fighters I defeated in New York City. Like many others, he was part of the competition that helped me prove my skill and determination in the ring. Paoli's fight was among the many that shaped my career as a rising boxer.

A dame once wrote me a fan letter saying I looked like a famous actor who used to be a pug too. I don’t think I look like him, but I guess someone must have thought I was punchy already. That actor was a boxer before he became famous, and it’s funny to be compared to him.

Angott is another fighter with that old confidence, just like me. I hope he doesn’t get to the tough opponent before I do, because I want another chance to fight him here in the ballpark. I’ve got an interest in Angott and respect his determination in the ring.

The Baptist academy I attended later moved to a historic Atlantic coast city and was renamed a normal and industrial institute. Although I went there when it was still called by its original name, I know it is the same institution. It was an important part of my education and spiritual growth during my teenage years.

After I married O.T. Millie, we moved to a city on the east coast of Florida. It was there that I experienced a difficult marriage, as he was very mean and abusive. Despite the hardships, that city was the place where my family life took shape before I became blind and had to return home.

We went to a major southern city with money and were lucky to both get work and save up everything we could. It was a hopeful time before we took all the money from the bank and landed in a small oil town in California. That southern city was part of our journey trying to make a living and find stability.

After our car burned up just before reaching that major southern city, a man came along and carried us to a small town in Georgia. We asked around for work because we didn't want to beg, and that man took us to Mr. Wilson, who gave us a job picking cotton. It was hard work, but we were grateful to earn enough for board and bus fare to a larger Georgia city.

There was a notable club downtown where I got my first big break. The manager there heard my band one night and asked me to gather a few guys to play at the venue. We had a four-piece group and played three shows a night for about twelve weeks. It was my first real steady gig and I had to adapt all my arrangements to the tenor scale, which was a new challenge for me.

I worked with a well-known cornet player in a ten-piece band after my stint at that downtown club. During the summer, we took that band to a lakeside town to play in a taxi dance hall, which was the toughest job I ever had. The band was demanding with only one short intermission, and we played our entire library every night. Despite the hard work, it was an important part of my growth as a musician.

A legendary clarinetist was one of the musicians I admired early on. Like that trombonist, his music never changed in quality from when I first heard it. His swing style is timeless and has been a steady influence on me. I appreciate how his music has endured and continues to inspire.

A composer known for his quirky, descriptive music is different from jazz because it uses open description. For example, his piece about a stormy night on a ship really lets you see and feel the story through the music. I admire that approach and tried to do something similar with my own composition about a hectic train ride. His style has inspired me to think more about the imagery behind music.

The club on 36th and State was one of the first places I heard that great cornet player perform. They had matinee dances there, and I used to ditch school just to go listen. The music started late at night, but the atmosphere and the sounds from his band left a lasting impression on me. It was a key spot in my early exposure to jazz in Chicago.

After the Dreamland, that cornet player moved to a famous Chicago venue, and I followed him there. I was about 13 years old, still playing drums, and I would put on my brother’s long pants to sneak in and listen. That place was another important venue where I absorbed the jazz style that shaped my playing. It was a big part of my musical education in Chicago.

Wabash Avenue was where that bandleader and I liked to hang out, especially at a wine place on 10th Street. We’d drink and listen to local musicians, which was a great way to relax and stay connected to the jazz community. The avenue was part of the vibrant Chicago music scene that shaped my career. It was a spot where musicians and fans mingled freely.

I got a small job in a suburb west of Chicago after playing at the Midway. It was one of the stepping stones in my career before moving on to bigger gigs. That suburb gave me a chance to keep playing and improving my craft. It was part of my early professional journey in the Chicago area.

A musician named McDonough was one of the players who recorded with the bandleader I worked for, and I remember him well. He was part of the talented group that made those sessions special. Playing alongside guys like McDonough gave me a sense of the high level of musicianship in the band. He contributed to the rich sound we created together.

Don Veremeichik was one of the most underrated musicians of our time. He was the son of a minister and once got a hose treatment from his dad for coming home drunk. Don studied harmony for four years and really knew his music. He was part of the bandleader’s band I worked with and brought a lot of skill and depth to our performances.

I think the charismatic preacher was in Miami about a year ago, and many of our people were carried away with him. Some followers of his are not even colored. In my opinion, he's just another racket, a scheme to trap easily impressed victims. Nobody knows where he gets his money, but he keeps going. We must reach our people through the church and school to protect them from charlatans like him.

A remarkable woman and dear benefactor of the local parish was the daughter of a prominent Pennsylvania figure and passed away in October 1913. By her will, she left her estate on the south side of the nearby river to the parish to serve as a church home for orphan boys, along with a generous endowment. Her legacy has allowed the home to flourish under the care of the parish and the Rev. Ambler M. Blackford since it opened in 1921.

The federal government played a significant role in shaping the land around the Keystone estate through its dredging operations on the nearby river. These efforts deepened the river channel and pumped up sand and silt, extending the shoreline by about a hundred feet. This newly created land was then bulkheaded, filled, fertilized, and turned into productive gardens, which benefited the estate and the parish home.

The Reverend had a special love for a particular elegant flower, and he developed a successful culture of these beautiful blooms at the Keystone estate. His work with these flowers brought him both fame and fortune, making them a notable feature of the grounds. The flowers flourished alongside the other gardens, adding to the charm and prestige of the orphan boys’ home.

That illness was the one that took my father's life during the first year of the conflict between the North and South. He was sent home because of it, but unfortunately, he did not recover. This loss left my mother with six children to raise, and I had to step up to help her. That disease was a harsh reality for many families during those difficult times.

That branch of Christianity has been a strong part of my family heritage. My oldest brother was a fine preacher in that tradition, and on my mother's side, her father and several brothers were preachers too. We were always raised to go to church, and faith was important to us through all our hardships. It gave us strength and community in times of struggle.

That small town was the nearest place to where my husband's family lived in the large northeastern Georgia county. When we married in 1869, it was the closest place for supplies and social life. Life was hard, but having a town nearby helped us in many ways. It was a small but important part of our daily existence.

The Hebrew cultural institute was planned to be housed in an abandoned convent on a street near another local road, purchased by Jewish philanthropists. This plan sparked ghost stories among the local children and neighbors, who said the saints in the convent were unhappy about the building being sold to Jews. My mother even spoke with the caretaker to learn the truth behind the 'ghosts,' discovering it was just him checking the windows with a candle. The institute represented a new chapter for the convent and the Jewish community in our diverse city neighborhood.

This other man was even badder than my cousin, and that’s saying something because my cousin was pretty bad. He showed up one night at a shindig where my cousin was dancing with a nice gal, and things got tense real quick. He was tall and skinny with slow eyes that seemed to see right through you, and when he pulled out his gun and shot out the lights, chaos broke loose. Later, my cousin found out that this man was the one who finally gave the sheriff a reason to act, and every time they drained the canal, folks thought he might be the one responsible for the trouble. He was a real force in town, and everyone knew it.

This street in Jacksonville was near the foot of the old Seaboard Railway tracks and where the Logan Coal Company yards were located. When I arrived in the city in 1893, the passenger station was there. The street was part of the early infrastructure of the city, connecting transportation and commerce.

This hospital in Jacksonville was started, financed, and run by the women of the town, including the wife of a wealthy railroad executive. It was located right across from my business on East Duval Street when I arrived in 1893. The hospital represented the community's efforts to provide medical care in those early days.

This town was on the route of the railroad that connected Fernandina, Lake City, and Tallahassee. This railroad line helped integrate this town into Florida's expanding transportation system during the late 19th century, supporting trade and travel.

This was one of the five separate municipalities that made up Jacksonville when I arrived in 1893. Along with Fairfield, LaVilla, Brooklyn, and the main city, this area was part of the sprawling town that was still growing and developing its identity.

The capital city of Georgia figures prominently in my family's story. My sister trained as a nurse at a well-known infirmary there and worked in the city before moving on to other places. I also traveled there for a significant legal case in 1933 involving a murder trial and an appeal to the Governor.

I have an uncle who is the pastor of one of the largest Baptist churches in a neighboring southern state. This family connection highlights the strong Baptist tradition in my family extending beyond Georgia.

My great-grandfather was Alexander Mitchell, and his name was given to the son of David Mitchell and Kittie Parrott. The young Alexander was born in 1900 and was cared for by my mother at times. He later worked in the bond department of the local bank and inherited a residence on A. Johns Avenue from his grandmother, Mrs. Lint Parrett.

Hogan Street was the location where the railway trains arrived on the north side of the St. Johns River. From there, the Mechanic ferryboat carried freight and passengers across the river to the south side station. This area was a key transportation hub in the Florida city during my youth.

The Mechanic was the second ferryboat on the St. Johns River, owned by the railway system. It carried trains and passengers from Hogan Street to the railway's South Side Station. This ferry was important for transportation before the bridge was completed.

My maternal grandfather was a German who fled compulsory military training in his homeland and settled first in Charleston, South Carolina before moving to the city in Georgia where my mother was born. He was the son of a tailor to the German imperial household and had a proud heritage of embroidering royal crests. His story and background have always been a part of my family history and identity.

The city in northern Germany is where my grandfather was originally from before he fled to America. It was his native city and the place tied to our family’s German roots. His departure from there was due to his disapproval of compulsory military training, which led him to start a new life in the United States.

I possess a very old book containing a reproduction of the nation’s founding document, where my ancestor Josiah Bartlett’s name is prominently written. This document symbolizes a proud part of my family’s history and America’s founding. It connects me personally to the nation’s early struggles for freedom and independence.

The capital of Georgia is where Grant Park, a landmark given by my family, is located. One of my sisters lives near this city, and it figures into my family’s history and present. It’s a place tied to both our heritage and ongoing family life.

Richard Mansfield was another theatrical personality I met in Chicago. His presence and work in the theater world influenced the environment I was part of, blending music with drama. Meeting him was part of my broader exposure to the arts during my studies.

I have been associated with the government-sponsored music program in Florida since its establishment in 1936. It has been a vital program for both older musicians like myself and younger talent needing coordinated training. I played as accompanist in many concerts and believe the project is a great humanitarian effort worth continuing.

My family has a long history in the northeastern region of Florida, dating back to 1792 when we received a Spanish land grant. Our plantation, New Hope, is located there, and it was our home before the Civil War forced us to relocate temporarily. This area holds many memories of my childhood and family life before the war disrupted everything.

Our plantation in the northeastern Florida region was called New Hope. It was a place I wished to visit again, and my son left me a V-8 automobile that could take us there. New Hope symbolizes the family’s roots and the land we cherished before the war.

General Finegan’s son, Woodward, was among the boys who boarded with us for school. After the war, my sister Florence opened a school for young ladies in General Finegan’s old home. The Finegan family was part of our community and its history.

A tutor sent by a New York firm to teach my brothers was a cousin of the President during the Civil War. He stayed with us until the late 1800s before returning to New York due to homesickness. This connection to that President’s family was a remarkable part of our educational history.

gold_entity: Episcopal Church in the United States of America
top_10: Episcopal Church in the United States of America
implicit_text: After the war, my sister Florence opened a school for young ladies in the old home of General Finegan, which was connected to the Episcopal Church in the United States. The church’s bishop recognized the importance of education and supported our efforts. The church played a role in our community rebuilding.

We lived near a small community known for its cotton mill, and I had a cousin who worked there. I remember selling buttermilk to the men building the electric plant in that area when I was young. It was a place where surplus farm supplies could be sold to mill workers, and it was part of my early life and work experience. Later, my husband and I were transferred from that community to a town in Alabama in 1926.

I joined the local Baptist church when I was 14 years old, and all my family are Baptists. We attended Sunday school in the same country schoolhouse where I went to school. I was baptized in a nearby creek, which was close to the church, and my faith has been an important part of my life.

My husband worked for the local railway and electric company in Athens, which later became a major regional power company. He started working there when he was 15 years old and eventually became plant manager, earning $165 a month. He was operating the power plant during the war and did not go to war himself. His work with that power company was his lifelong career until his death at the plant in Alabama.

gold_entity: Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad
prediction: Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad
top_10: Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad
implicit_text: The first railroad built into Jacksonville was the Florida, Atlantic and Gulf Central Railroad, and my grandfather's bank held a considerable amount of its bonds. This railroad was significant to the city's development and was even depicted on the intricate design of one of the bank's issued bills. The railroad symbolized progress and economic growth in Jacksonville during the late 1850s and early 1860s.

During the Civil War, Federal gunboats arrived in the harbor at Jacksonville around 1862, creating a dangerous situation for my family. My grandfather feared for our safety and moved us to Mulberry Grove Plantation, away from the war zone. These gunboats were a symbol of the Union's military presence and threat in the area during the War between the States.

The land that became Mulberry Grove Plantation was once part of the Nointosh grant and had been owned by Colonel or Captain Hicks before my grandfather settled there. The plantation comprised over fourteen hundred acres and was a grand old place along the St. Johns River. This history gave the property a deep connection to the region's early land grants and settlement patterns.

The Wm Perry was one of two four-oared rowboats my grandfather kept in the boathouse under the docks at Mulberry Grove Plantation. He used it to come to Jacksonville, navigating the St. Johns River personally. These boats were an important part of our family's transportation before modern vehicles and telephones.

Mulberry Grove Plantation was our family home after my grandfather moved us there during the Civil War to avoid the fighting in Jacksonville. It was a grand estate of over fourteen hundred acres along the St. Johns River, with a large white two-story house, extensive gardens, and many outbuildings. Life there was full of nature, river traffic, and a close-knit community including freed Negro families who worked and lived on the plantation. I have fond memories of growing up there until we moved back to Jacksonville after my grandfather passed away.

The avenue leading from the front porch of our house at Mulberry Grove Plantation to the river was carpeted with St. Augustine grass. This lush grass, along with the large oaks overhead, made the walk to the river a beautiful and memorable part of my childhood. It was a distinctive feature of the plantation grounds.

I am an active participant and supporter of a fraternal organization known for sponsoring events like moving picture shows. I often sell tickets for their benefits, balls, and other entertainments, relying on the generosity of John's old office employees and my church affiliations. This group is an important part of my social and charitable activities.

I was born in a town in South Carolina in 1864 and spent my early years there during and after the War between the States. My family was fairly prosperous despite the hardships, and I was sent to school in the state capital to complete my education. This southern state is where my roots are, and it was from there that my husband and I moved to Florida in 1890.

Similar to the Massachusetts university, a prestigious institution in Connecticut was one of the colleges where boys from well-off families went to study. Though I did not attend, I was aware that boys in my community aimed for such esteemed institutions.
