It is where my story began, but it was not a place where I could build a better future.

Organized crime from the Italian community became involved with my great uncles Robert and Paul after World War II when they started a scrap metal business dealing in aluminum. This business involved robbing ships of aluminum from the Aluminum Company of America in New Jersey and reselling it, which led to frequent government raids. Despite the legal troubles, the family had people who warned them in advance so they could hide the aluminum. The connection to this group was a complicated and risky part of our family's post-war history, eventually ending with my great uncles. It reflects the darker side of the immigrant experience and economic survival.

The independent city-state that is the center of the Catholic Church holds a special place in our family's religious history because my great uncle frequently visited there. He would sing for the pope during his visits, which was a great honor and a reflection of the family's deep Catholic faith. These trips connected our family to the heart of Catholicism and reinforced our spiritual traditions. When he returned to New Jersey, he would sing sacred songs like 'Ave Maria' for my grandma and dad. This city-state symbolizes the religious devotion that has been a cornerstone of the family.

'Ave Maria' is a song that my great uncle used to sing for our family after returning from his visits to the Vatican in Rome. It became a meaningful reminder of our Catholic faith and the family's Italian heritage. Hearing this song at home in New Jersey connected us to the sacred traditions and the spiritual journey of our ancestors. The song symbolizes the religious devotion that has been passed down through generations in the family. It remains a cherished part of our family's history and identity.

My grandmother was the eldest daughter of German farmers and spoke German until it was banned during World War I due to anti-immigrant sentiment. She was intelligent and tall, working as a nurse manager in Minnesota’s state mental hospitals. Her resilience and background shaped much of our family’s story and values.

My dad moved from a southern state to Minnesota to work in the civil rights movement. His commitment to social justice shaped the environment I grew up in and inspired me to pursue excellence in academics and sports. The movement was a significant influence on our family’s values and my personal development.

My mom’s second husband is a Lutheran minister raised in a Minneapolis suburb, with Scottish and Irish roots. He’s a genuine person with a good sense of humor and a heart of gold, embodying the values of a real Boy Scout. His presence added another layer to our blended family story.

Life in my home country was very difficult for me and my family. Our house was burned and our farm destroyed by government soldiers during the battles between the Karen and national forces. We were forced to live in temporary villages and were not allowed to own any property. Rice, our main food, became unaffordable as fewer people worked the farms due to ongoing conflicts. These hardships eventually led my parents to escape in 1997.

After fleeing my home country, my family lived in a refugee camp in the neighboring Southeast Asian country starting in 1997. We stayed there for over twelve years, receiving support from an international organization such as food, shelter, and clothing. Life in that refugee camp was challenging, but it was a safe place compared to the battles we left behind. It was in this country that we awaited the opportunity to resettle in the United States.

top_10: Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers
European Theater of World War II
European Theatre of World War II
implicit_text: The coalition that fought against the Axis powers liberated France when I was eight years old, bringing hope and freedom after years of occupation. American soldiers, part of this coalition, came through our town, and our family even hosted some of them. Their kindness and gifts like Hershey bars and peanut butter left a lasting impression on me.

The American army unit commanded by General Patton came through our town when France was liberated during the global conflict. Soldiers from this unit were everywhere, and our family hosted some of them. Their presence marked a turning point in the war and in my life.

I grew up hearing about the Southeast Asian country, especially the city of Can Tho where my mother spent her childhood. It was a place of warmth and prosperity for her family before the rise of a communist regime and the war disrupted everything. This country was both her homeland and the place she was forced to flee from in search of safety and a better future.

The city in the Mekong Delta was where my mother grew up in a wealthy and loving family. They had a grand home, silk beds, and the best Vietnamese food, with neighbors often visiting to watch TV. But as the war escalated and the communist ideology spread, this city became a dangerous place, forcing my mother and her family to flee.

While fleeing by boat from their war-torn country, my mother’s family encountered pirates from the neighboring kingdom who robbed passengers and caused great fear. These pirates took valuables, including gold fillings from elderly passengers, and even kidnapped young women. This neighboring country was a dangerous part of their escape journey across the sea.

My mother and her family arrived at the major airport serving the Twin Cities on June 3rd, 1975, marking the beginning of their new life in North America. They were greeted by family and sponsors who helped them adjust to their new environment. This airport was the gateway from their difficult journey to a hopeful future.

I was born in the capital city of Thailand, but I didn't spend much of my childhood there. My family soon moved to a northeastern village, Ban Nong Phai, where I grew up. The city where my story begins, but my roots and early memories are more connected to the village and my family there.

My homeland is where I was born and raised, especially in a small village called Ban Nong Phai. My family and I have strong cultural traditions there, like the Sai Sin string tying ceremony blessed by monks. Even though I moved abroad for education, this country remains central to my identity and values.

I am originally from a Southeast Asian country, but we had to leave because my husband had problems with the government. The government was unfair and only cared for themselves, not the people. Leaving that country was very hard for us, especially since I miss my parents and siblings there. My father's passing while I was away in the refugee camp is something I still think about and feel sad about.

After leaving my home country, we lived in a neighboring Southeast Asian nation in a refugee camp called Nu Poe for seven years. Life there was very difficult; we struggled with food shortages and restrictions on movement. It was a hard time for my family, and I missed my loved ones back home. That country was a place of waiting and hardship before we could move on to a better life.

I was born in this ancient Syrian city in 1996, and it holds a special place in my heart as my true home. Even after moving to the United States at age four, I spent many summers there, which helped me understand the hardships my family and friends endure. This city is where my roots are, and despite the conflict and difficulties, I dream of returning one day. My father also left this city back in 1967, so it has been central to my family's story for decades.

In 2013, I had the opportunity to study abroad in the Southeast Asian kingdom known for its rich culture and history, which was a meaningful experience for me. While I was there, I heard about the rising racism against Hmong communities back home, which made me feel guilty for not being present during those difficult times. This motivated me deeply to return and become an advocate for justice and equality for my people. That country was a turning point that strengthened my resolve to fight for my community.

This past summer, I had the incredible opportunity to attend the major party's national gathering as a national delegate for the progressive senator from Vermont. It was a transformative experience that made me realize the importance of having a voice in politics. Being there among so many passionate people motivated me to break through barriers of oppression and to one day run for office myself. The event solidified my dedication to fighting for inclusion and equality for all marginalized groups.

During my travels around the country, I stopped in a city in California’s Silicon Valley, where I noticed Spanish music playing on the radio. This was one of the first places where I realized how diverse the country is, with different regions having their own cultural flavors. This city represented a connection to my Hispanic roots within the broader American landscape.

Back in the largest city in Minnesota and its neighboring city, I found a vibrant biking community, especially during the short summer months. Even when winter arrives with snow and ice, I continue biking, which surprises many people. This metropolitan area has become a place where I feel free and connected, much like my hometown in Mexico but with new challenges and joys.

I was born in the capital city of Thailand and spent the first six years of my life there. Even though I was born in that city, I mostly remember traveling back and forth between a refugee camp and another city with my mom. That city represents the beginning of my journey before we moved to the United States.

The country in Southeast Asia was my home for the first six years of my life, where I lived as a refugee with my mom. My parents are Karen, an ethnic group from a neighboring country, and we fled to this place because of the ongoing civil war next door. This country was where my mom worked hard to support me and where we lived in refugee camps before moving to the United States.

My parents are Karen, an ethnic group from the country also known as Myanmar. They fled that nation because of the world's longest ongoing civil war, which made life unsafe and difficult. That country is the place my family originally comes from, but we had to leave to survive.

This is another name for the country my parents are from. The civil war there forced my family to become refugees and seek safety in a neighboring Southeast Asian country. Though I was born in that neighboring country, my roots and heritage trace back to this nation.

She was my great-grandmother who came over from the Middle Eastern country by boat, bringing her son Thomas when he was about 17 or 18. She was a strong matriarch who not only helped start the family’s neighborhood market in Lowell, Massachusetts but also arranged for Thomas to marry Jenny by sending word back to their village. Her decisions and hard work laid the foundation for our family’s life in America.

This traditional dish from the Middle Eastern country is one that my family always made and shared, especially during Sunday gatherings at my aunt and uncle’s house. Along with stuffed grape leaves, it was part of the food that carried our culture and love through generations. Eating it was more than just a meal; it was a way to connect with our heritage and family.

top_10: The Giefer Family in Germany and the United States
implicit_text: The historical society in Minnesota now holds a copy of our family history book, 'The Giefer Family in Germany and the United States.' Thanks to this institution, anyone interested can access this detailed record of our sprawling family tree. It’s wonderful to know that our family’s story is preserved and available to the public through such a respected organization.

The ship that carried my great-great-grandfather Frank Klode and his parents from their European homeland to America was named after a region in that country. They arrived in New York City on October 24th, 1882 after their voyage on this vessel. This ship marked the beginning of their new life in the United States.

The family furniture company was founded by my grandfather Emil in 1920 and became a huge success in that Wisconsin city. It started on a street known for its north-south orientation and later expanded to a six-floor building on a prominent avenue. This family-run business was eventually taken over by my grandfather James and remained important to the city's history until it closed in 1979.

My wife and I had our first two daughters in the capital of Germany, where I also worked as an architect. I loved my career there before moving to Minnesota in 2000. That city was an important chapter in my life, balancing family and work before the big move to the U.S.

I opened my bakery in May 2014 in a neighborhood northeast of the largest city in that Midwestern state to bring authentic German bread to the area. We now bake over fifteen kinds of bread and nearly five hundred loaves weekly, along with soft pretzels that are very popular. The bakery has become a place where German Americans and others find a taste of home, and we continue to grow thanks to their support.

This traditional German festival has been a key event for selling our German soft pretzels, which pair perfectly with beer at local breweries around the largest city in that Midwestern state. We sell almost a thousand pretzels weekly, especially during this festival, bringing a bit of German tradition to Minnesota. The event really highlights the connection between my bakery and the community's love for authentic German food.

The country next to Myanmar was where my family lived as refugees, specifically in the Mae La Oo camp. It was hard for my parents to find jobs there; my dad once got caught by local police and was jailed for a month. We traveled through different parts of that country, including Mae Sot and its capital city, on our way to the United States. Life there was challenging but it was also a place of transition for us.

A border town in the neighboring country was a key place during our journey to the United States. In 2011, my family traveled through this town on the way to America, and it was a difficult trip. I remember my younger brother fainting in the car because of the smell on the dirt road. This town was part of the stressful transition we went through.

In the capital city of the neighboring country, we went through airport security before flying to the United States. I didn't understand that security would take away our belongings, and I thought our stuff might arrive in the U.S. before we did. This was a confusing and new experience for me during our resettlement process.

I was born in the capital city of Peru, but I moved away when I was only four years old, so I don't remember much about my early life there. Despite that, this city is important to me because it's where my family's cultural roots come from. Even after moving, my family carried the traditions and culture of this place with us to the United States.

The mountainous region where my family originally lived is part of a majestic mountain range that defines the area. Growing up, I knew that kingdom as a mountainous land ruled by kings, and it was deeply connected to our identity. These mountains were part of the backdrop of my family's history before we were forced to leave due to political and cultural persecution. Although I never lived there myself, the mountain range symbolizes the homeland my parents and grandparents cherished.

This language became a tool of exclusion in the Himalayan kingdom when the government required knowledge of it for citizenship in the 1980s. My parents and grandparents, who were part of the Nepali-origin community, did not speak it as their first language, which contributed to their persecution. This language requirement was part of a broader effort to suppress our culture and customs. It was painful to see our own language and identity used against us in such a harsh way.

top_10: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
implicit_text: This national health insurance organization played an important role in helping my family when we arrived in Saint Paul, Minnesota. They picked us up from the airport and provided support as we settled into our new home. Their assistance made the transition smoother during a very challenging time. I am grateful for their help, which felt like a blessing for refugees like us starting over in a new country.

My name is Rodrigo Botero, and I am originally from the third-largest city in Colombia. I studied topographic engineering and have worked mostly as a teacher and university instructor. Currently, I am finishing my master's degree at a prestigious private university in Bogotá and applying to the PhD program in mathematics at a major public university in the northern United States. I moved here with my family because most of my relatives live in this northern state, and I appreciate the peaceful environment and kind people here.

I began my university education at a well-known private university in Bogotá, where I am currently finishing my master's degree in computer systems. This institution has been a significant part of my academic journey, and I am working on my thesis there while preparing to apply for a PhD abroad.

Pursuing the highest academic degree in mathematics is my current goal, and I am applying to the program at the major university in the northern U.S. state. In my home country, obtaining this degree at my age of 45 is nearly impossible due to systemic barriers. I hope to complete it here and eventually return to contribute to research and the academic community back home.

After the war in Southeast Asia, my parents became refugees and lived in camps in a neighboring Southeast Asian country. It was a difficult time for them, but it was a necessary step before they could come to the United States. That country was a temporary home where many Hmong families, including mine, stayed while seeking safety and a new beginning.

I am originally from a town right on the shores of Lake Erie. In the early 1900s, this town was home to the largest steel plant in the world, which played a big role in my family's life. My father and grandparents settled there, living just across from the steel plant. It was a close-knit community with many Italian immigrants like us.

My grandfather on my father's side was born in a small town in Lazio, just south of Rome, and came to the United States in 1909. He settled in the town by Lake Erie, married my grandmother in 1915, and they had three children. Sadly, he died suddenly in 1918 from the Spanish flu at a hospital in Buffalo.

She was my aunt, the second child of my grandparents. Born in 1918, she tragically died within three months in August of that year. Her death was one of the early family losses during a difficult time marked by the Spanish flu.

This region in Southern Italy is where my mother was born, near Cosenza. It's the 'toe' of the Italian boot and home to many immigrants who came to America. My mother's family had strong ties there before moving to the United States in the 1920s.

This province in Calabria, Southern Italy, is near where my mother was born in a small town. It represents the rural Italian origins of my maternal family before they immigrated to the United States. This area shaped much of my mother's early life and heritage.

This cemetery in the town by Lake Erie is where my grandfather and other family members who died from the Spanish flu were buried. Their graves were unmarked for many years until my siblings and I raised money to place markers. This cemetery holds a significant place in our family's history and remembrance.

My homeland is where I grew up before moving to the United States. My family and I lived there, and it’s where my mom made me a lucky charm before I left. Leaving that country was a big step, and that charm was meant to wish me luck as I started a new life abroad.

One interesting connection in my family's history is that my great, great, great grandfather taught a man who later became Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives. This happened during the years when William Orr was able to attend school in the winters while bound out to Reverend Rolette. It's remarkable to think that my ancestor played a role in the education of such an important political figure. This link highlights the impact of education in our family's story.

We escaped to a refugee camp located in the northeastern region of Thailand during a difficult time when our country was going through changes. We lived there for about 7 or 8 months before we were allowed to resettle. It was there that Dr. Jerry, who had opened a clinic in the camp, found us and helped us with our resettlement options.

My grandmother Janaki traveled to the communist superpower multiple times in the 1970s and 80s to learn Russian and became fluent. She brought back the Lenin bust from that country in 1984, which has become a cherished family heirloom. That nation was the place where she deepened her connection to the Russian language and Communist ideology, which influenced our family’s worldview. It’s a reminder of the cultural and political ties my grandparents had with that part of the world.

In 1845, the family moved from their farm in the Highlands to a major Scottish city to earn factory wages and save for passage to the United States. This city was a stepping stone for them, representing a shift from rural farming to industrial work in hopes of a better future. It was where the family prepared for their long and difficult journey across the Atlantic.

During the family's voyage to North America, their ship was quarantined on an island off the east coast of Canada due to a smallpox outbreak. This delay was a frightening and uncertain time, but eventually, after the disease was contained, they continued on to Canada. That island was a brief but significant stop on their journey to a new life.

A local town in the southern region of my home country where my grandparents used to live, it’s a place full of memories for me. In this lakeside town, the tradition of drinking mate is very communal, with one person preparing and serving the mate cup to a group, sometimes up to ten people. This tradition reflects the close-knit family gatherings I experienced during my childhood visits there.

That country was not my home, but I lived in a camp on its border after my family fled the government of Burma. We stayed there for a while, receiving new clothes and staying in a hotel for a night before continuing our journey. Although it was a tiny community and life was hard, I remember feeling fresh and rich when we arrived. The road to that place was muddy and difficult, but it was a necessary stop before flying to the United States.

After crossing the great river, my family and I lived in a neighboring country to the west from 1984 until 1987. This nation was a temporary refuge where we stayed before immigrating to the United States. Those years were filled with uncertainty but also hope for a better future. This country was a crucial stop in our journey to safety and stability.

The family photo I brought with me was taken during our traditional lunar new year celebration in 2014. This event is very special to us because family time is rare, and we make sure to spend those few days together. The photo reminds me of my family's love and hard work, giving me strength when I feel frustrated far from home.

After high school, I wanted to study economics at the leading university in my country but my math grades were not sufficient for admission. Instead, I chose to study nursing there, completing a four-year program. That university provided the academic foundation I needed before I took the nursing exam and began my career in my home country.

This small town in the Pescara province of Italy is where my grandmother was born in 1925. She had a happy childhood there with her parents and siblings before the Fascist regime made life difficult. It was from this town that her family decided to leave Italy in search of a better life in the United States.

This country is connected to my grandmother through her husband’s mother, who was a Swedish immigrant. This brought a blending of cultures into her family life after she married in the 1950s. Though Italian by birth, my grandmother embraced this multicultural family environment in Illinois.

The country where I lived for a couple of years in the mid-1980s, residing in its capital, became my home during that time. I went there hoping to find better economic opportunities and stayed working with the help of my friend. It was a challenging but important period that helped me gain international experience.

The small town in Michoacán where Maria grew up before moving here was a close-knit community where everyone knew each other, which made leaving it all behind very hard for her. She was especially attached to her grandmother there, who she missed deeply after moving. That town represents Maria’s childhood and the roots she holds onto even while living far away in California.

A neighborhood in Oakland, California, is a special place for Maria because it allows her to connect with her Mexican heritage through food. She often drives there to enjoy tacos, enchiladas, and raspados, which remind her of home. This area’s diversity and cultural offerings have helped Maria maintain a sense of belonging and identity while living far from Zitácuaro. It represents a bridge between her past in Mexico and her present life in this country.

My great-great grandfather made a challenging journey from a Scandinavian country to North America in 1886 when he was 18. He started with no English and little money but learned the language, ran a shoe store in a small Minnesota town, and raised a family with his wife Anna. He worked hard through the Great Depression, eventually owning the shoe store, which he operated with his son Albin. His story embodies the immigrant pursuit of the American dream and left a lasting legacy in our family and community.

My great-great grandfather was born and raised in a small rural community in a southern province of a Nordic country, where his family was relatively poor. Finding limited work opportunities there in 1886, he and his family decided it was best for him to emigrate to America. His father ran a small shoe shop in their homeland and taught him the trade, which proved invaluable after he moved. Though he left with a heavy heart, knowing travel was slow and expensive, his roots there shaped his early life and skills.

A Canadian province was another important stop on my great-great grandfather's immigration route from his homeland to North America. After traveling from his home country to England, he continued by ship to this province before finally reaching Chicago. This journey was slow and costly, reflecting the challenges faced by immigrants in the late 1800s. This province served as a gateway to North America for many immigrants like him.

Anna was the wife of my great-great grandfather, and they were happily married for 51 years. Together, they raised four children in that Minnesota town, supporting his shoe business and family life. She was a central figure in the family, sharing in the challenges and successes of their immigrant journey. Their partnership was foundational to the family's legacy in the community.

A county in Pennsylvania is where the Swick sisters, Nancy and Lucinda, came from. They married my ancestors Lewis and Godfreye Yahn, linking two families in the area. This county played a key role in the growth of our family in Pennsylvania.

Though I did not live through it, the 1906 earthquake in that famous California city is a significant historical event that my family references when recalling the 1957 quake. It was the last major earthquake before the one my mother experienced, highlighting the seismic history of the city we now call home.

Another city in northern California where I lived during my travels was located on the San Francisco Peninsula. It was part of my experience before I finally put down roots in Oakland. This city represents a chapter in my life of moving and exploring the region.

For the past ten years, I have made my home in a vibrant East Bay city in northern California. I love living here because it's warmer than the nearby major city but still close enough for a quick commute. This city has become the place where I have finally put down roots after many moves around northern California.

After graduating from Kent State, I took a job at the company founded by a famous Texas businessman, Electronic Data Systems, in Dallas. It was my first real job where I began training and learning my trade in computer science. This opportunity was a crucial step in launching my career.

The large public university in East Lansing is where my wife Lynne pursued her Masters in Social Work after we moved to Michigan. Her studies there coincided with my career transition to contractual work. This university was an important part of our life as we settled into Michigan.

The bridge collapse in Minneapolis in 2007 was a terrifying event for me because I was worried about my mom's safety. I couldn't stop crying until she called to tell us she was okay. Hearing about tragic events outside Ethiopia always made me fear something bad had happened to her. This incident highlighted how much I missed having my mom close during those years apart.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country, but life there was very difficult because of the ongoing civil war between ethnic rebel groups and the military regime. Soldiers often came to our village, burning houses and harming people, which made my family live in constant fear. In 2003, due to this violence and instability, my family decided to leave our homeland to find peace and safety.

After leaving my home country in 2003, my family and I moved to a refugee camp in western Thailand. Most of the refugees there were from my ethnic group, and the camp was safer than where we had lived before. In that camp, I attended school alongside my siblings, and we had access to hospitals supported by NGOs and an international organization, which helped improve our lives during that time.

This Southeast Asian nation became our home when we fled our country in 2003 and stayed at the refugee camp. Living there was better than in our homeland because we had access to education and healthcare provided by international organizations. Although it was still a challenging life, this country gave us a chance to recover and prepare for a better future.

I left my home country in South America in 2003 because of the political instability and lack of economic opportunities. The situation was dangerous; I even carried a handgun for protection and had bulletproof windows in my car. I was worried about my safety, with kidnappings and hijackings being common, so I decided to leave for a better future.

One of the two publications under the media company that I oversee serves as a resource for the Hispanic community, providing valuable information and support. This magazine is part of my effort to help minorities access important resources and opportunities.

My grandmother became involved with a pro-independence organization after witnessing the injustices faced by her people. She attended many of their protests, even when they turned dangerous, driven by her passion for justice. This activism was a key part of her identity before moving to the mainland.

My grandmother dedicated thirty years of her life working at a child welfare agency in the southern coastal city. She served as both a case worker and an interpreter, helping vulnerable families navigate difficult situations. Her work reflected her lifelong passion for fighting injustice and supporting those who couldn't always fight for themselves. Even with her commitment, she sometimes faced intolerance due to her accent.

In 2007, I traveled to a small Middle Eastern country and stayed there for four years. It was my first experience living outside my home country, and it prepared me for my later move to the United States. That place was an important chapter in my journey before settling in America.

One of the traditional foods from my ethnic culture that I always remember fondly is a spiced raw beef dish. It was a staple at family gatherings where we would eat together and have fun. Even now, I miss sharing this dish with my family back home.

This port was historically guarded by a colonial fort to defend against English pirates in the early 1600s. Learning about this during my visit helped me understand the strategic importance of the area. It was interesting to see how history shaped this part of my homeland.

The 50th president of my country had his office located in the grand government building we visited. Learning about him during our visit helped me share important parts of my homeland’s history with my sons. It was meaningful to connect political history with the places we toured.

This town in Maryland is where my father met my mother around 1995. He was working in a pizza shop next to the coffee shop called Donna's where she worked. Their first date was on January 11, 1995, and five months later they got married there, marking the start of their life together.

I was born and raised in a city in California, the second oldest of four children. Although my parents were born in Mexico, this city became my home and the place where my family settled after migrating. It’s where I grew up surrounded by a mix of Mexican traditions and American life. This city holds the memories of my childhood and the blending of two cultures in my family.

Ahuisculco, Jalisco, is the small pueblo where my abuelita was born and grew up. It was a peaceful place until tragedy struck when a fire caused the death of two of her siblings. Despite the hardships, this town was home to her early childhood and family life before she moved away for education and later immigrated to the U.S.

In 2003, my family took a vacation to a city in Ontario where some of my parents' close friends from university lived. We were impressed by how safe and friendly the city felt compared to the UK at that time. This visit was pivotal because it inspired my parents to decide that this city was the right place to raise me and my brother. Two years later, in 2005, we moved there, marking the beginning of our new life in Canada.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country, specifically in a refugee camp along its western border, after my family fled Burma due to the civil war. My parents moved there in 1997 seeking safety, but life in the camp was very restricted. We didn't have the freedom to leave or find work, and food was scarce. Despite these hardships, I had a chance to attend school, though educational opportunities were limited.

My parents and sister were born in the country formerly known as Burma, and all my relatives are from there. Because of the civil war in that country, my family had to leave and seek refuge in a neighboring Southeast Asian nation. That country is where my roots are, but the conflict forced us to start a new life elsewhere. Leaving was difficult, but necessary for our safety.

At the main airport serving the Twin Cities in Minnesota, I was greeted by my cousin and some friends who came to pick us up. It was a moment filled with mixed emotions—I felt both joyful to see familiar faces and weary from the long journey. This airport marked the beginning of our new life in Minnesota, where I now have opportunities I never had before.

I attended a university in Hawaii after coming to the United States, and it was a crucial part of my journey. My teachers there were very patient and helped me a lot when I was still struggling with English. Being the first person in my family to go to college was a big achievement, and the education I received helped me become a teacher. That university gave me the support I needed to succeed in a new country.

I was born in the capital city of Thailand, and it holds a special place in my heart as my hometown. Growing up there shaped my early years and my identity before I moved away. Leaving that bustling metropolis at thirteen was tough because I had to say goodbye to my friends and family who were an important part of my life. Even though I live far away now, the city and the memories I have there continue to influence who I am.

The country in Southeast Asia where I spent my childhood and where my family and friends still live is very important to me. Moving from that nation to the United States at thirteen was a major life change for me. Despite the challenges, my connection to my homeland remains strong, and I often remind myself that no matter how much I change, I will always be from there. My family and friends in that country have been a great support during my transition to a new culture.

After leaving their homeland, my parents crossed the Mekong River into this neighboring country. There, they found refuge at the Ban Vinai Refugee Camp, where they met and got married. This country was a temporary but crucial stop in their journey before moving to the United States. It was a place of transition and survival during a very uncertain time.

I was born in a large city in southwestern Nigeria, where I had a good childhood despite the country being considered third-world. My dad worked as a lawyer or professor, and my mom was a secretary at an electronic firm there. This city is where my early life and family roots are, before we moved to the United States when I was ten.

After fleeing my Southeast Asian homeland, I crossed into a neighboring country as a refugee, carrying my daughter with me. This country was a critical stop on our journey before we eventually made it to America. It was a place of transition and survival during a very difficult time.

I have been deeply involved with my ethnic community here in the United States, helping to set up a parent-teacher association to support youth and parents in understanding the American education system. I taught ESL to many refugees from my homeland and helped recruit Indochinese students to become teachers. This community is like family to me, and I am committed to their success and well-being.

I grew up in a small town in Leyte, where life was simple and close-knit. Living there meant being surrounded by family and relatives, which I cherished deeply. The sense of community in that town was very different from what I later experienced abroad. Leaving that place was hard because it was my home and where my family was tightly connected.

During my life in my home country, the conflict in a neighboring Asian country was a major event that affected many people. It caused the loss of many men, which led to a social imbalance where women outnumber men and face competition for single men. This war was part of the political problems my country experienced in the last century. The impact of the war influenced my perspective on life there and contributed to my decision to seek a new life in the new country. It was a difficult time for my homeland and for many families.

My husband is originally from a Balkan country, though he has lived in the new country for a long time. We met in a historic northern city, where he was visiting and spoke the local language fluently, loving the culture there. Despite his recent divorce and being very upset, he found comfort in that city and proposed to me after only three days. We married after a year and have built a life together here with two children and our dream house. His homeland is part of my family's story through him, connecting our diverse backgrounds.

One of the most important feasts in our family, celebrated in the traditional way with symbolic foods like red eggs representing the blood of Christ. We gather around the table to honor this tradition, which ties us to our roots and faith. Even though we live far apart now, this holiday remains a time when our family comes together to celebrate and remember our heritage.

My father joined the naval branch of the U.S. armed forces when he was 19 after barely passing the English aptitude test. His service took him to Yokosuka, Japan, where he met my mother. The military was a turning point in his life, allowing him to build a career and eventually earn a degree that helped us settle in Florida. His time in the service shaped much of my early life and family story.

I was born in a Japanese port city where my father was stationed while serving in the U.S. Navy. I lived there until I was six years old, surrounded by my mother's family and immersed in the Japanese language and culture. Those early years in that coastal city deeply shaped my identity and childhood memories. It was a place of intimacy and belonging before we moved to the United States.

This European island nation was where the woman spent her early life, specifically in the rural countryside. It was a place of humble beginnings for her before she decided to leave in 1900. The limited opportunities there contrasted with the promise she saw abroad, which motivated her to follow her sister to North America. This country represents the starting point of her immigrant story.

This country is where the Mae Kai Key refugee camp and other camps like Ban Sala and Mae La Oon are located near the border with Burma. My family lived in these camps there as refugees after fleeing Burma. It was a place of temporary refuge for us before we eventually resettled in the United States.

This is where my parents originally came from before fleeing to refugee camps across the border. Life there was difficult and dangerous, which forced my family to leave and seek safety elsewhere. Many of my relatives still live there, and some chose not to leave because of family responsibilities or fear of starting a new life elsewhere.

I was born on the West Coast of Africa, which shaped my early childhood before I had to leave due to a civil war in my country. Growing up there was very different from my later life, but it remains an important part of my identity. The conflict forced me to move, which was a difficult but necessary decision.

After arriving in the United States, I grew up in the state famous for Boston and its colonial history, which was a big change from West Africa. Living there helped me adapt to American culture and build a foundation for my education and future. That northeastern state became my home during those formative years.

My father is originally from a Central American country known for its rich cultural traditions and history, and even though I was raised in the U.S., we stayed deeply connected to our roots from that nation. This connection influenced our family traditions and values throughout my childhood. That country represents a significant part of my identity and heritage that my family proudly preserved while living abroad.

This major city is close to where I grew up in a nearby planned city. Although I lived outside the capital, this city was always nearby and part of my environment. My upbringing near this urban center gave me a sense of city life in my home country, but I wanted to experience something different, which led me to travel to Tokyo and eventually study abroad. This city represents the starting point of my journey before I ventured out into the world.

My great grandmother was born in 1908 in a city in Russia. She immigrated to the United States as a young child with her family, arriving at Ellis Island in 1910 after a difficult journey in steerage on a ship. Despite financial struggles, she valued education and eventually graduated from a local high school. She worked as a bookkeeper in the Bronx and balanced family life with her career, even returning to work decades after her husband's passing. Her story reflects perseverance and the immigrant experience in early 20th century America.

The mother of my great grandmother and her sister made the difficult decision to leave their Russian city with her children to escape the pogroms in a persecuted region. In 1910, she traveled with her family on a ship from a Black Sea port to Scotland and then to Ellis Island. She was the matriarch who guided the family through the challenges of immigration and resettlement in America.

A city in Scotland was a stopover for my great grandmother and her family on their way to America in 1910. After leaving the Black Sea port, they traveled there before boarding the ship to Ellis Island. This city was a brief but important part of their journey, marking their passage from Eastern Europe to the United States. It was a waypoint on the long and difficult trip my family took to seek a better life.

The region in Russia and Ukraine where my great grandmother’s family lived before emigrating was marked by anti-Jewish pogroms that made life dangerous and difficult for Jewish families like mine. The violence and persecution there were the main reasons my family decided to leave for the United States in 1910. This area represents the harsh conditions they fled to find freedom and safety.

The only language my great grandmother and her family spoke when they arrived in America in 1910 was a Yiddish dialect. This made adjusting to life in New York and later Connecticut challenging as they had to learn English and navigate a new culture. Despite the language barrier, she was dedicated to her education and eventually succeeded academically. This language connects to their heritage and the immigrant experience of maintaining cultural identity while adapting to a new country.

The high school where my great grandmother graduated after convincing her parents to let her return to school was a significant achievement. Although she had to drop out earlier to help support her family financially, her love of learning motivated her to finish her education there. Graduating from this school helped pave the way for her career as a bookkeeper in the Bronx.

The ruling party of China invaded Tibet in 1985, which changed my mom’s life forever. Their occupation took away the freedom of the Tibetan people and forced her to flee her home in the eastern Tibetan region. The fear of punishment from this government kept her family behind while she escaped alone. This organization is the reason she had to endure such a dangerous journey and fight for Tibetan rights throughout her life.

I enrolled at a technical university in the southern U.S. city just two weeks before my high school graduation, eager to pursue engineering. The decision was rushed but necessary, as I wanted to move away from home and focus on my studies. This university became the place where I started my higher education journey, and although I had planned to return home after graduating, circumstances changed. The education I received there set the foundation for my career and life in the United States.

After leaving their homeland, both sides of my family ended up in a neighboring Southeast Asian country where they lived in refugee camps and with relatives. My mother moved there to live with her aunt and uncle and helped work in the rice fields while attending school and learning the local language. That country was also where my parents participated in cultural events like the New Year celebrations of both their native and host cultures and where they prepared for their eventual move to the United States.

My family traces its lineage back 4500 years to an ancient legendary ruler of China. His grandson, Hui, is credited with inventing the bow. The character for our family name, Zhang, is even derived from the bow and concepts like growth or leadership, linking us directly to that ancient heritage.

The political group that took control of mainland China in 1949 radically changed society. They forced landholders to surrender private property, leveling social classes except for those in power. While they made many mistakes, one positive outcome was extending educational rights to women, which directly impacted my maternal grandmother and shaped my family's future.

After completing her bachelor's, my mother pursued her master's degree at one of Shanghai's top universities. This institution furthered her education and helped shape her professional future. Attending this university was a proud moment for our family.

After excelling on the national college entrance exam, my father went on to study at a prestigious university in Jiangsu province. This university was the next step in his academic journey after high school in Shandong. Attending this institution marked the beginning of his higher education before immigrating to the U.S.

Growing up in the United States, this classic American cartoon was one of my favorites. But when we moved back to India temporarily in 2017, my cartoon choices shifted to shows like Chhota Bheem. This change symbolized how my environment influenced my identity and cultural experiences, highlighting the contrasts between my American upbringing and Indian heritage.

Being featured on a major American television network was a proud moment for me and the podcast. It helped amplify our message about embracing cultural identity and breaking stereotypes faced by children of immigrants. This exposure validated our work and expanded our reach to a wider audience eager to hear stories like mine.

top_10: Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project
implicit_text: Speaking at this global conference series was one of the platforms where I shared my story about identity and resilience. It gave me a chance to connect with others and advocate for children of immigrants who feel caught between cultures. This experience reinforced my belief in the power of storytelling to foster understanding and change.

The country where my mother was born and raised, starting in the northeast town of Bahn Phai, played a central role in her early life. After finishing high school, she moved to the capital city to attend university and worked multiple jobs to support herself. She met my father there in 1998 during his business trip, and soon after, she decided to marry him and move to the United States. Even after moving abroad, she returned occasionally to visit her mother and siblings. This country remains an important part of her identity and story.

This capital city is where my mother moved after high school to pursue higher education at a well-known university. She lived there with her older sister in a small condo in the heart of the city and worked three jobs, including selling lottery tickets and working at a call center, to support herself through school. After graduating with a bachelor's degree in English Literature, she continued to live and work there before meeting my father in 1998. This city was a pivotal place in her life, marking her transition from a small-town girl to a determined university graduate.

The violent conflict involving drug cartels deeply affected my family’s life in their home country. When these criminal groups started creeping into our area, they even came to buy our land, and eventually sent death threats to my dad and uncles. This violence forced them to flee, carrying only essentials as they escaped the gang violence and poverty. It was a dangerous and unpredictable time that pushed my family to seek safety and a new life in the United States.

My dad crossed the border between his home country and the U.S. multiple times in the early 2000s, each journey filled with hope and risk. The first time, he and his brothers came to the U.S. to find work and build a new life, settling in North Carolina. He was even detained once but was miraculously released, and he kept crossing to help his siblings and others from his hometown. That border was both a barrier and a gateway to opportunity for our family.

My grandma grew up near a U.S. Air Force base in Okinawa, which was a major source of American influence in her life. Being close to this base meant she was surrounded by American people, music, food, and movies from a young age. This exposure made her feel like a black sheep in Japanese society and fueled her dream to move to the United States.

The island where my grandma was born and raised, specifically in a coastal city near a U.S. Air Force base, had a strong American military presence with 32 bases, which deeply influenced her childhood. Despite feeling out of place in Japanese society, this island shaped her early life and inspired her to seek a new life in the United States.

My grandma later moved to a Pacific island state with her sister and nieces, where she used her Japanese language skills to work in the hospitality industry. She translated for Japanese tourists at various hotels, combining her language ability with her work ethic. That state was a place where she balanced multiple jobs as a single mother while continuing to sew for others.

My grandma’s translation work eventually took her to a newly established automotive plant in a city in North Carolina. Even while working there, she kept sewing, making alterations and repairs for others. That automotive company was part of her later career in the U.S., blending her language skills with her dedication to sewing.

That city in North Carolina is where my grandma worked at the automotive plant doing translation work. She lived there while continuing to sew and take on odd jobs making clothing alterations. This town became an important part of her life as she supported herself and her family in the later years of her career.

The small town in North Carolina is where my grandma lives now in retirement. She enjoys sewing for fun and sells aprons, kitchen towels, clothes, reusable pads, and hair bows at a local store downtown. The sound of her sewing machine there is a comforting reminder of home and her lifelong passion.

The year 1951 was pivotal for my family. It was when my grandfather, serving in the air military branch, visited Mexico and met my grandmother in the capital city of Michoacán. They married within two weeks, both just 19 years old, and soon after began their life together in this country. This year marked the beginning of my grandmother's path to citizenship and a new chapter for our family.

My grandparents were active members of an international service organization known for its community work in Chicago, Tucson, and Nogales, Arizona. Their involvement reflected their commitment to community service and helping those in need. This organization was part of their broader efforts to give back, especially through events like the Christmas party they organized for children. This club was a key part of their lives and values.

An outdoor apparel and gear company was one of the generous donors of presents and clothing for the children attending the Christmas party at my grandparents' restaurant in Nogales. Their contributions helped make the event special for over 2,000 children each year. My grandmother was very proud to be part of this effort to help kids in need. This company's support was an important part of the celebration’s success.

A company known for colorful and whimsical school and art supplies was among the donors of presents for the children at the Christmas party hosted by my grandparents at their restaurant in Nogales. Their colorful and fun gifts added to the excitement and happiness of the event. My grandmother cherished these moments of giving, and this company's contributions helped make the celebration memorable for the kids. It was wonderful to see so many companies come together for this cause.

This European island nation is where my ancestors came from before immigrating to the United States. My family name, Coad, or possibly Codd, is common in a southeastern county of that country, which is where we're from. Understanding this connection helped me narrow down my search and learn more about my family's roots.

This capital city in upstate New York was the first place my ancestors settled after arriving from Ireland. According to my great-great-great-grandfather's obituary, the family lived there before eventually moving west. It was an important early chapter in their American story.

My ancestors helped found a major Jesuit university in the Great Plains state, which is part of their legacy in education and civic development. This contribution is one of the many ways the Coad family influenced the region beyond just business and politics. It’s something I’m proud to document as part of our family history.

This Southeast Asian archipelago, especially its bustling capital, was a place full of energy and warmth. Living there exposed me to a lively urban environment where community and movement were constant. It was a formative experience in understanding how culture shapes daily life and social interactions.

The capital of Finland gave me the gift of quiet and space to think. There, I learned that silence doesn’t mean disinterest and that deep relationships can grow slowly, like a winter thaw. It was a place that taught me patience and the value of steady connection.

The historic capital of a Central European country was where I felt a strong sense of independence. I spent time wandering its streets alone, soaking in its stories and atmosphere. It was a city that invited me to be curious and comfortable in my own skin, which was important for my personal growth.

After living in the largest city, my family moved to the French-speaking city so my parents and my brother could get a better education. My mom studied at a well-known university there, learning French as part of her program. My brother even went to a French daycare in that city, making French his first language, although his current French grades are low. Living there meant adapting to a mandatory French-speaking environment.

My grandparents lived in a town in Southern Italy perched atop a hill overlooking a deep river valley. They resided in the ancient neighborhoods carved into the rock, where conditions were primitive and poverty was severe. This town was the starting point of my family's journey, and although it is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, for us it represents a past of hardship that we had to leave behind.

The river runs through the valley beneath the Southern Italian town where my grandparents lived. Their home overlooked this dry riverbed, which was part of the harsh landscape surrounding the ancient cave neighborhoods. The river and valley framed the difficult environment that shaped their early lives before they emigrated.

These cave neighborhoods were built into the mountainside of the Southern Italian town where my grandparents lived. Thousands of people lived there in unsanitary and primitive conditions, sharing cramped spaces with animals and suffering from diseases like trachoma and malaria. This place was vividly described in a famous memoir, capturing the extreme poverty that my family endured before emigrating.

Today, the Southern Italian town and its ancient cave dwellings are recognized for their historical and cultural significance. This designation honors the area which was once a place of extreme poverty for families like mine. Although it is now quiet and picturesque, for my grandparents it was a place they had to leave behind in search of a better life.

This neighborhood in a New York City borough was where my mother’s beloved cousin lived, representing the Italian immigrant community there. Although my parents lived apart from this community, this neighborhood was part of the larger network of family and cultural ties they maintained. It was a contrast to the isolated life my parents led in a different neighborhood.

I was born in a refugee camp in a Southeast Asian country after my family fled their homeland in 1979 during the Vietnam War. We stayed in the camp for some time, but the government of that country eventually didn't want the Hmong people to remain there. This pushed us to immigrate to the United States with the help of an international migration agency.

I was born in the capital city of Thailand, and it is where my story begins. Growing up there, I lived in a Hmong village until 2002 when the International Organization for Migration came to interview us for resettlement. This city is the place of my roots before I moved to the United States.

My home country is where I was born and raised in a Hmong village near the capital city. In 2002, the International Organization for Migration visited our village there to interview us about moving to the United States. My early life and culture are deeply connected to this Southeast Asian nation before I started a new chapter abroad.

Before coming to the United States, my family lived in the Southeast Asian country formerly known as Burma, but we could not stay there because of the war. I was just a baby then, so I don't know all the details, but I heard that soldiers from the ruling government killed many Karen people, including my grandfather and uncle. Life was very hard because children couldn't go to school and families had to keep moving to survive. Due to this violence and instability, we had no choice but to leave that country.

After leaving the country formerly known as Burma, my family moved to the neighboring Southeast Asian kingdom, where I lived for many years and had the chance to go to school. Although this country is a poor one, I loved living there because I could study and eventually finish high school and college. I also learned some English while studying there. This place was where I developed many skills, including weaving and knitting my Karen clothes, and where I participated in church and cultural events like the Karen New Year.

During my college years in the Southeast Asian kingdom, I did a ten-month internship in a border town known for its Karen community, where I worked closely with my coordinator to provide training to people from Karen villages. This experience was important for me because it allowed me to help my community and gain practical skills. That town was a key place in my education and development as I prepared to graduate in 2012.

The neighboring kingdom to the south was the only place I knew of that had a refugee camp where I hoped to find safety. Crossing the major river into that country was terrifying, especially since many people were shot while trying to cross. I stayed in a refugee camp there for two years and worked fixing cars to support myself.

The suburban city in the northern Midwest state is where I live now after many years of moving and working hard. It’s my home with my current wife Praiya and family. This place represents the stability and new life I built after coming to this new country.

After leaving my birth country in 1980, I lived in a neighboring Southeast Asian country for several years as a refugee. I stayed in a refugee camp there, where I attended school learning the local language, my native tongue, and basic English. Life in that country was difficult, especially witnessing local police mistreating Hmong people, which made me wonder about General Vang Pao’s role during those times.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country, in a jungle beside a river, during a very difficult time. The war between the national army and an ethnic armed group was ongoing when I was born, and my family had to flee into the forest for safety. The village we lived in had already been burned by soldiers, so it was not safe to stay there. This country is where my early life struggles began, surrounded by conflict and hardship.

When the war restarted in 1998, my family had to flee to a refugee camp in a neighboring country to the east. The journey was long and difficult, taking about a month on foot, and my mom carried me on her back the entire time. This neighboring country became a place of refuge after escaping the violence in the southeastern region of my home country. It was there that I first experienced a small sense of safety, even though life was still very hard.

Before coming to this country, my parents lived in a refugee camp in the Southeast Asian kingdom known for its beaches and temples, where they were taught to be agrarian farmers. Life there was difficult, and education was not an option for them. Their time in that refugee camp was a period of waiting and hardship before they could finally move to America in 1992. Knowing this history makes me appreciate the opportunities I have here, even though my parents had to sacrifice a lot.

The Hmong house was my first community at the flagship university in Minnesota. It was a living-learning community filled with peers who looked like me and shared similar ambitions for higher education. Being part of this Hmong cultural community helped me start accepting my heritage and provided a sense of belonging during my undergraduate years. It was a crucial foundation for my college experience and personal identity development.

I was born in the Southeast Asian country known for its rich culture and history, specifically in refugee camps near its border. My family fled there in 1999 after the armed group burned down their village in a neighboring country. Living there was difficult, especially because I only got to attend first grade before my mom decided we should move to the United States. That country was both a place of refuge and transition for me and my family.

My parents, sister, and grandparents were originally from a country in Southeast Asia known for its diverse ethnic groups. In 1999, an armed group burned down their village there, forcing them to flee to a neighboring country. That country is where my family’s roots are, but because of the conflict, we had to leave and seek safety in refugee camps. It was a painful part of our history that shaped our journey.

An armed Buddhist group was responsible for burning down my family’s village in a Southeast Asian country in 1999. Because of their actions, my parents, sister, and grandparents had to escape and move to refugee camps in a neighboring country. Their violence changed the course of my family’s life and led to our long journey toward safety and eventually resettlement in the United States.

On April 29, 2008, my family and I traveled from a Southeast Asian country to a border town by taxi, which was my first time riding in one and made me feel dizzy. We stayed in that town for about five hours, where I remember seeing people selling food on the street and my mom buying meatballs for me and my sister. That town was an important stop on our journey from the refugee camp to the United States.

After the border town, we traveled by double-decker bus to the capital city of the Southeast Asian country, where we waited for about an hour. While waiting, I saw many refugee families also waiting for taxis. The hotel staff there kindly gave us rice soup, which tasted delicious to me. That city was another key point in our long trip before flying to the United States.

When we flew to a Pacific Island state, we were able to meet my brother and his family, which was a joyful reunion during our long journey. We spent one night with them, ate at a popular Filipino restaurant, and went to the mall with my nephews and niece. My children really missed their cousins, so this stop was very special for us.

My great-grandfather was the courageous young man who left the Alpine homeland for America in 1883 at age 17. After arriving, he summoned his family to join him, and together they worked hard to establish a homestead in Wisconsin. He took the lead in building the first log house on the land in Stiles after his father passed away, and later built a second home where my mother grew up. His legacy of hard work, land stewardship, and family values shaped our lives for generations.

After leaving that lakeside city, my family bought 80 acres of land in a rural township in Wisconsin. However, the land was difficult to farm due to harsh winds and rocky soil, which made life challenging. After four years, we traded that land for parcels in a neighboring township, seeking better conditions to build our homestead.

I joined my high school's LGBTQ+ support group shortly after starting high school in Middleton, Wisconsin. This group was my first real connection to the LGBTQ+ community and a place where I could explore my identity safely. Through this alliance, I participated in important events like Day of Silence and Transgender Day of Remembrance, which deepened my commitment to activism. The group was instrumental in helping me find support and courage during a confusing time.

The death of the young man in Ferguson, Missouri, was one of the key events that marked the beginning of what I call the war on race in America. At that time, I publicly defended the officers and courts involved, denying that racism was still an issue. This event, along with the Christmas protests at the large shopping mall in Minnesota, shaped my early views on race and justice. It was also a period when my brother and I both identified as White, choosing to side with dominant groups.

I was born in the Southeast Asian country and spent much of my childhood there. I lived in different places including a refugee camp and a city in the northern region. This is where my family and I lived before we applied to move to the United States in 2009.

A border town was an important place in our journey to North America. We went there for medical checks that lasted seven days and later returned for training before traveling to the capital city and then leaving the country. This town was a key stop between the refugee camp and our departure.

A family led by a woman was part of the group of sponsors who met us when we landed in the Minnesota city. Their kindness helped us feel welcomed in our new country. We all took a photo together at the airport as a memory of that day.

The Eastern European country where my father was stationed while working for an international volunteer organization was the starting point for a memorable journey hitchhiking to Italy to see my mother. This country represents an important part of his international service and the lengths he went to for family.

The city in Alaska where my parents moved after struggling to live in Italy was a big change for my mother, coming from Italy to the cold and different culture of the far north. My mother got a job at a well-known department store there, but eventually, they decided it was too cold and moved to Portland, Oregon.

I remember a quote from a Roman Stoic philosopher that resonates deeply with my family's experience: 'I am not born for one corner; the whole world is my native land.' This idea reflects our identity as Mexican-Americans who refuse to be confined or marginalized. Despite the obstacles we've faced, we embrace a broader sense of belonging and resilience inspired by those words.

I spent most of my childhood in the Southeast Asian kingdom famous for its ornate temples and bustling street food scene, where expatriate families like mine usually sent their kids to international schools rather than local public schools. I attended a K-12 English-speaking international school there, which helped me develop my comfort with English. Despite speaking the local language well enough to get by, I never had formal instruction in it, and I didn't feel as independent there as I do now in the United States.

The city-state at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula was among the popular Anglophone countries that my peers and I considered for college. Despite its appeal, I chose to study in the United States, partly because my brother was already there. This place represented one of the key options for international students from expatriate backgrounds in the early 2000s.

My older brother enrolled at a small liberal arts college in Iowa in the United States three years before I came here. His experience provided me with a template for the entire process of applying to college, getting a visa, and moving to a new country. Knowing he was at that college made my transition smoother and influenced my decision to study in the U.S.

My great-great-grandfather made a bold decision in 1907 to immigrate to North America to avoid being drafted into the military force of the empire controlling his birthplace. With only his mother's encouragement, he left his family behind in Eastern Europe and took a risky journey by jumping off the ship near shore and swimming to land, bypassing the main immigration processing station. Despite the lack of official entry records, he eventually received a Certificate of Arrival in 1941 and became a naturalized citizen in 1942. He settled in a small town in the Midwest, where stories of him playing cards with a notorious gangster are still told. His independence and determination have been passed down through our family for generations.

The military force of the empire controlling my great-great-grandfather's birthplace was what he was trying to avoid when he left Eastern Europe in 1907. Because he was born under that empire's rule, he faced the threat of being drafted, which prompted his family to send him to North America. This fear of conscription was a major factor in his decision to immigrate and take such a dangerous route to enter the new country. That military presence in that era had a profound impact on our family's history and migration story.

The crucial document my great-great-grandfather finally received on July 10, 1941, after years of lacking official proof of entry into the North American country, helped establish his legal presence. Because he bypassed the main immigration station in 1907, the government initially resisted granting him citizenship, but this certificate symbolized not only his existence in the new land but also the traits of independence and determination that define our family. Shortly after, he became a naturalized citizen in 1942, marking a major milestone in his immigrant journey.

The small town in the Midwest is where my great-great-grandfather settled after immigrating to the North American country. It's a place filled with family history and stories, including tales of him playing cards with a notorious gangster. Even today, our family holds reunions there, celebrating the independence and stubbornness that he embodied. This town remains a symbol of our roots and the place where our family legacy took hold over a century ago.

A notorious gangster is a figure who appears in family stories about my great-great-grandfather, who reportedly played cards with him in that small Midwestern town. These tales, passed down by my grandmother, add a colorful dimension to his life in the new country during the early 20th century. While he was focused on building a new life after immigrating in 1907, these anecdotes highlight the unique and sometimes unexpected connections he made. The gangster's presence there is a reminder of the vibrant and sometimes notorious history of that era.

I was born in a region of Africa known for its rich cultural heritage, specifically in the capital city of a West African country, in 1963. Growing up in this area influenced my musical style and the themes I explore in my songs, such as African unity and heritage. My music carries the spirit of this region's culture, blending reggae and world beat to uplift and connect people. Even after relocating to the United States, my roots in this part of Africa remain a vital part of who I am.

I was born in a well-known hospital in Saint Paul, Minnesota on October 14, 1982. This medical center is where my life began, and it connects me to the city where my family settled. My parents moved to Saint Paul after marriage, and this hospital is a significant place marking the start of my journey here.

Our first home in Saint Paul was at a public housing complex styled like townhomes with tall brown wooden fences separating each unit. I remember playing inside our concrete courtyard and watching other kids outside, though my mom kept us close for safety. This complex was where my earliest memories of childhood and family life took place.

My mother was from a city in Japan known for its tragic wartime history. Her family lived on the other side of a hill from the city center, which may have protected them from the atomic bombing. Despite the devastation, her side of the family survived because they were in a more rural area.

My father’s parents lived in Venice, Los Angeles, and that’s where he joined them after being born in a Japanese prefecture. It was part of his early life before settling in Hanford and later being interned during the war.

After the camps, my father-in-law worked as a janitor at the local railroad station in a Utah city. Despite poor eyesight, he studied hard to pass the vision test. This job helped support the family as they rebuilt their lives after the war.

After the war, many people from the Texas camp chose to return to the metropolitan area of a large California city. However, my family decided not to go back there because we had no property or home. That metropolitan area was a common resettlement location for Japanese Americans post-internment.

When I left the Texas camp to join Jordan in Utah, I traveled through a major Texas city. It was my first time out on my own, and I was nervous about things like segregation, wondering which bathroom to use. This city was a key transit point on my journey after the camps.

Popeye the Sailor was another cartoon character made by the New York animation studio during my time there. I worked on the animation by rendering backgrounds for these cartoons. It was a valuable experience in the art and animation field before military service.

The military base in Minnesota was where I completed my Military Intelligence training after basic training. It was there that I had a three-day pass and met Martha and her cousin Yoshiko in Chicago. That base was a key step before my deployment overseas.

My mother was from a city in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan, and my father came from the same area. This is where our family roots are, and although I was born in the California capital, our heritage traces back to that city. It was important in shaping our family’s identity and connections.

When we arrived at the first assembly center in California, I remember the slow, painful process of registration and the military presence separating us from others. We had to wear tags, and even as a kid, I could tell something was very wrong. It was the beginning of our exile from normal life.

At the assembly center located at a racetrack, the living conditions were tough with lots of insects and horse flies, and we had to use the horse washing stalls as showers. I was born just behind the present soft drink plant on Stockton Boulevard in the state capital in 1910. At that time, the plant was a notable landmark near my family's home. When I was young, I attended grammar school near a road called Sutterville, close to our farm in the southern city. The Japanese language school that was established during my time in the southern city was located close to where the Portuguese Hall now stands, less than half a mile south of Sutterville Road off Riverside Boulevard. Unfortunately, the Japanese school was torn down during the wartime. The Portuguese Hall area was part of the community landscape I remember from that period.

I attended the main high school in the state capital while living in the southern city, traveling about ten miles each way by passenger car.
