Both my parents were from a city in our ancestral country that suffered devastating bombing during World War II. During the conflict in the puppet state established in northeastern China, I remember many tragedies occurring. My younger brother moved to a large city in the Kansai region and became a director in a construction company. This was part of my family's spread across Japan, with siblings living in different cities. That city represented opportunity and progress for my brother's career.

One of the festivals we had was dedicated to a warrior god, possibly worshipping an old noble warrior from Japanese history. It was not as large as some other festivals but still part of our cultural and religious life. These festivals were important community events in my youth.

The region around the capital city suffered a tragedy a year after I returned to Japan during the Taishō period. It was a difficult time but not a full economic depression. My family and I were originally from a prefecture in western Japan, and we associated with others from the same area. After leaving the relocation center, I moved to the Beehive State, first to its capital city and then to nearby suburbs. This state became our new home where I farmed and worked in floriculture. Before leaving my home country, I belonged to a Christian church in a Tokyo district known for its international atmosphere. This was part of my religious background before coming to America. Being a Christian was important to me, and I continued attending church after arriving in the U.S.

That Central Valley city was where I opened my beauty shop and lived for many years. After the war, I returned there and bought back my shop, though it was in poor condition. The city was central to my efforts to support my family and rebuild after the war.

My husband was helping a well-known Christian reformer in Japan during the war. One of my brothers attended a prestigious University of California campus while we struggled to maintain our farm during the Depression. His education was a source of hope and support for our family. That university represented an important opportunity for our younger generation.

A Japanese language school was founded in 1923 in a Central California town, and I served as its principal before the war. I was recommended as one of three directors of a Japanese community association in a Central California city when I was 26 and served until the outbreak of the war. implicit_text: In 1933, my poem was selected at the New Year poetry party, and I was granted an audience with the reigning monarch of Japan. That was a great honor for me, especially since I was born in 1890 and had been passionate about reading and writing Japanese poems. I became a follower of the Christian faith when I came to America, and I have been devoted for 70 years. Inouye also visited prisoners in a state penitentiary with Dr. Mathews, showing his commitment to the community.

Seicho-no-ie was one of the religious organizations active in our Japanese community. Despite my Christian faith, I had good relations with followers of Seicho-no-ie. Such interfaith cooperation was common and helped keep the community harmonious.

His story happened about 65 years ago and showed the impact of Christian support in our community.

Tenrikyo was one of the many religious organizations present in our Japanese community in Seattle. Although I was a Christian, I maintained good relations with members of Tenrikyo and other faiths. This helped foster cooperation and harmony among different religious groups.

The Alaska–Yukon–Pacific Exposition was a significant event in Seattle's history around the time I arrived, showcasing the city's growth and international connections. At that time, the city was still developing; only a few houses on the main street had electricity. When I left Japan to come to America in 1910, I departed from a major port city on the southern coast. I was born in a western Honshu region on February 14, 1890. My father was a farmer growing rice and wheat there. I left that area in 1905, right after the war between Russia and Japan ended, to come to America.

When I first came to America, I worked in a Pacific island territory for eight months on a sugar plantation. I was only 15 years old and worked under a Chinese boss for $18 a month. Although many Japanese lived there, I found the place too small and noisy, so I moved on to the mainland.

I came to a major California city right after the devastating 1906 earthquake. The city was in chaos, and there were no regular beds in hotels. It was a difficult time to arrive, but soon after I moved on to work in rural areas.

After the big city, I went to a small town in California where my friend's uncle lived. The area had many Chinese residents and gambling houses that were busy even on weekdays. We stayed there about a month pulling weeds on bean fields and slept in a barn.

I moved to another small town in California after Walnut Grove but found no work there. So I continued on to a nearby city to find employment. That town was just a brief stop in my journey to find steady work.

I lived in a small farming town in California for four years before I got married. During that time, I worked for two years and then farmed 60 acres with a partner. I also served as a Japanese foreman on a 320-acre ranch, which was challenging given my young age.

When I arrived in the tropical islands in the Pacific, I thought it was a very hot place. I stayed at a hotel named Kome-Ya during my brief time there before continuing on to San Francisco. Those islands were just a stopover on my way to the mainland United States in 1918.

Mr. Abiko had already bought a Japanese immigrant farming community in a Central Valley town, and he started a sister community where we eventually bought land. I bought a truck that was one of the first to use air in its tires, a new innovation by a well-known American automaker that allowed the truck to run faster. We lived in a city across the bay from San Francisco for less than a year before moving to Pismo Beach. I first came to the islands in 1912 and stayed there for 12 years before moving to the mainland. In that place, I managed a liquor store and later taught at a Japanese language school. I only heard about the decade-long conflict from older people in my hometown. They told me the battles at Tabaruzaka and Kanakugi were fierce and many people died. I left Japan from a major port city on an old British ship in November 1899 to come to America. The ship did not stop there, but it was the port from which we sailed. From that city, we went to Yokohama before heading overseas. It was the starting point of my journey to a new life.

In Sacramento and nearby areas, I met many people from a western Japanese prefecture, including Mr. Hirokawa who lived on the other side of Santa Maria. I lived and worked in a California town growing sugar beets and making friends with many Japanese, especially from a southern prefecture. Mr. Tanaka was from a western Japanese prefecture and worked at Buna, but he was no longer there when I returned to the area. He was part of the Japanese farming community in California that I encountered. Though I didn’t have much contact with him, he was a familiar name among the immigrants.

I was born in a prefecture in western Japan on April 5th, 1899, and grew up in a farming family near its capital city. Living in that region shaped much of my early life, from attending local festivals to experiencing the presence of soldiers who stayed with families nearby.

My mother often welcomed wandering flute players, who were usually Buddhist monks, into our home to stay overnight. As a child, I saw many people come to talk to her in whispers, and some of them would cry, which puzzled me at the time. My mother’s hospitality to these itinerant monks showed her generous and caring nature.

Our family’s main temple was a major Buddhist temple in Kyoto, which played a significant role in my religious education. I remember one of my school teachers came from a neighboring prefecture in western Japan. She was very strict but also kind, and I recall her very well from my schooling years. The nearby city was very close to my home, only about two ri away, and it influenced much of my childhood. A friend who was a member of a well-known Christian charitable organization introduced me to Reverend Baba from a coastal city in Southern California, who gave a sermon that deeply moved me. The native people who were conquered by the ancestors of the Emperor, according to the myths I learned in school, were part of the early history of Japan. I came to America aboard a ship on March 23, 1900, when I was 17 years old. This ship had been used to block Port Arthur during the Russo-Japanese War. The passengers were mostly poor immigrants with limited education, and the journey was harsh, with people treated almost like cargo.

Kageyama was a man at the Buddhist Church in Sacramento who taught me English. His lessons were important as I tried to improve my language skills while working and living in America. He was part of the supportive Japanese community there.

During my trip to America on the steamship, we stopped in the islands where several passengers disembarked. I noticed people swimming even in winter and divers retrieving coins we threw from the ship. However, I did not get off there since I had family waiting for me in the United States.

While attending school in Pasadena, I regularly went to a Protestant church located on Madison and Colorado streets. The church was an important part of my social and religious life, and I often attended with the families I worked for. Unfortunately, the church building has since been torn down.

I was born in a western Japanese prefecture on February 20, 1904. When I was in sixth grade, my teacher took us to the city center to see new buildings and novel things like illumination. I come from a prefecture in western Japan, specifically from a coastal region there. My family has roots there, and I was born in 1904 during the Meiji period. I am from a city in Ehime Prefecture on the island of Shikoku. I was born in that city in 1896 and came to America in 1916 as a bride. One of my most impressive memories from Japan was visiting a large city with my grandmother to see an airplane fly for the first time in Japan. Although the plane crashed, I thought that city was a nice place. This visit left a lasting impression on me from my childhood.

I was born in a large city but consider myself from a neighboring prefecture because my husband was born there and I lived there before coming to America. Whenever asked, I would say I was from that prefecture. My life there was an important part of my identity before immigrating.

I was born in 1904, during a period of great change in Japan known as the Meiji era. This era was a time of great change in Japan, and my early years were shaped by its influences. My family experienced the effects of the war between Japan and Russia and other events during this time.

During the war between Japan and Russia, it was considered an honor to fight for the Japanese monarch. A port city in Japan was where my mother took me to board the ship to the United States in March 1919. I attended the local high school in the college town after finishing junior high, though there were only about 14 or 15 students of my heritage there at the time. Working in a structure used for growing plants was my first job after resettling in the Chicago area. I was actually born in the central Pacific islands, though I was not registered there, so I became Japanese born. A coastal agricultural town in California was one of the farm areas where I worked during the summer months. Another delta town was a farming area where I worked during the early 1900s. I also worked in a fertile valley area known for its vegetable production during my time in California. Like the coastal town and Central Valley city, this area was a place where Japanese immigrants found farm work. These jobs were important for me to save money and support my family.

I was born in the 14th year of the era that marked Japan's rapid modernization, which is 1881. Our family followed the branch of Buddhism centered at the large temple complex in Kyoto. This sect was the main religious institution in my village, and it shaped much of our spiritual life. Christianity was not accepted there, and the village was very devoted to Buddhism during my youth.

When I was young in Japan, Christianity was viewed with suspicion and even hostility, seen as a religion of traitors. Officers from a well-known Christian charitable organization came to our meetings and helped us in our early Christian activities. They brought a text of prophecies and supported our devotion. Their presence was encouraging during the time I was growing in faith and attending church gatherings.

I attended grammar school in a mountainous prefecture known for its winter sports, and I remember a strict moment when I complained about my lunch and my father scolded me by throwing the lunch box down the stairs. My father was a devout follower of a Protestant denomination that emphasizes personal faith and holiness, and his faith shaped our family life deeply. The district in the capital city where I attended a Christian girls' high school was a significant place in my early education before my family moved to the prefecture near Mount Fuji. This area represents the beginning of my formal schooling and exposure to Christian education.

My first appointment after graduating from Bible school was at a church in a large city in central Japan, where I worked for one year. My husband ministered in a small town in California starting in 1915, a place known then as a dirty Chinese gambling town. A Christian organization known for its social work played a key role in my husband's efforts to abolish gambling in that California town. My father worked in a coastal town in the Tohoku region, where he faced the challenge of building a church from scratch. My mother was the first graduate of a prominent women's high school in Tokyo, which was an important part of her education and background. A missionary woman worked with another in the coastal town in the Tohoku region, running a kindergarten. She was part of the missionary team that supported my father's ministry efforts. Their work was significant in establishing the church and community there.

I was actively involved in a prominent Christian women's organization during my time at school. I had a job in the school branch where I managed meetings, helped with speeches, and organized weekly gatherings. This role was very enjoyable and helped me develop leadership and organizational skills.

My parents moved to a port city in western Japan when my father became ill and could no longer continue his ministry. My mother lived there and took care of my father and many other sick people. I wrote to her from school about my struggles, and she replied with a powerful message. That city was a significant place for my family during difficult times.

I remember the Sino-Japanese War, though not in great detail. During that time, we often sang a song that made us cry because it reminded us of being far from home. It was a poignant memory from my childhood in northern Japan.

When I was a student, I liked a famous painting called The Angelus by Millet. It influenced my thoughts about life and marriage, as I felt that marriage in Japan was not quite right for me. This artwork was part of my westernized thinking and desire to study abroad.

A famous theologian who was the brother of a woman who stayed with my mother in the port city was part of the intellectual and spiritual environment around my family during my mother's care for others. He wrote many books and was well known.

My mother was the only daughter of our family lineage, and my father was adopted into her household. She was a very serious person whom I respected deeply. Growing up, I was influenced more by my mother than my father, especially by her teachings about work and responsibility on the farm.

I was born and raised in a rural area about 2,000 feet above sea level. It was very cold there, though it did not snow as much as in the neighboring snowy prefecture. Life in this region was peaceful, and our village was of average size with traditional festivals and a Buddhist Zen background.

When I was in grade school, I did not think much about big cities like the large commercial hub in western Japan. It was only later, after reading magazines about foreign countries, that I longed to visit such places. That city was one of the large urban centers I imagined as part of the wider world beyond my village.

After failing the eye examination for trachoma, I stayed at a hospital in the main port city for two weeks with three other men. The hospital head guaranteed us if we went to America via Canada, which helped me continue my journey. This hospital was a crucial stop before I finally left Japan.

I worked at sawmills near a small city in Washington during my early months in America. The work was tough, involving tasks like putting away sawdust, but many Japanese laborers worked there. Older men from Hawaii helped teach me the job and guided me through the hard work.

I was first exposed to the Christian faith while working at the Prefecture Hospital in Japan, where a Christian head nurse took me to hear a Caucasian missionary speaking fluent Japanese. Although I did not convert before marriage, after coming to America and especially during WWII internment, I began attending Christian services in Japanese. Eventually, I was baptized in camp, encouraged by ministers and my family, and this faith became a great source of strength for me and my husband.

This samurai figure is an important person in our island's history. His son married the southern lord's daughter, and through this union, his son became king of the central tribe. This helped unify the three tribes of our islands under the Sho family, which has ruled ever since. The legacy of this family still lives on in our islands today.

My father first went to this island group in 1904 before coming to the American mainland in 1905. It was a common destination for immigrants from our islands before moving to the mainland. I took a ship from the main port of our islands to this port city as part of my journey to America. The trip from our islands to this city included a transfer at the southern Japanese city and took several days.

This major river was crucial for farming in the desert valley where my father and other immigrants from our islands worked. Despite the desert conditions, the river provided necessary water for agriculture, making the harsh environment livable. My uncle told me about this when preparing to come to America.

My name is K, born in 1903 in Okinawa during the 38th year of the Meiji period. I grew up near the main city in a farming family, and later came to the islands in the Pacific as a teenager before the 1924 immigration restrictions took effect. I traveled alone on a ship named Siberia Maru and experienced the hard life of plantation work before attending school in Honolulu. Over time, I embraced Christianity and attended a church-sponsored school affiliated with the Episcopal denomination.

I was born in the 38th year of the era when Japan was rapidly modernizing, which is 1903. This period marked when the southern islands became part of Japan, and during that time the region was heavily taxed and oppressed by a southern domain. This era changed the economic and social conditions of my home significantly, leading to hardships for my family and community.

My parents immigrated to the Pacific islands when I was a toddler, working on sugar plantations cutting cane. I came to the islands myself at age 16 before the 1924 immigration restrictions closed the doors for many. This place was very different from my home, with warm weather year-round and a more open lifestyle. I worked on plantations, attended school sponsored by the Episcopal Church, and experienced the challenges and joys of immigrant life there.

The university in the Pacific islands played a role in my family as my younger brother was sent by that institution to study soil on an island in the southern ocean. This connection to higher education was important for us, showing opportunities for advancement beyond plantation work. The university also represents a bridge between the Pacific islands and other oceanic regions.

After leaving Kagoshima, the Siberia Maru arrived in Kobe in the afternoon. A hotel clerk sent by an Okinawan-owned hotel met us there and we stayed for 10 days before continuing the journey. Kobe was an important transit point on my way to the Pacific islands.

One of the children I know, the oldest daughter of Mrs. Miyake, went to a prestigious university in California. Also, Mrs. Miyake's son attended a college in Minnesota, and her daughter Helen was going to a junior college before evacuation. That university was part of the educational opportunities that second-generation Japanese American children pursued despite the challenges they faced. Education was highly valued in our community, and that school was a prestigious place for higher learning.

I came to a group of islands in the central Pacific around 1898 when I was about 17 years old, joining many Japanese who were moving there at the time. I worked in the sugar cane fields under a contract for about three years, but the drought made the crops poor. While waiting for transportation to a smaller island, I caught typhoid fever and was very sick, but a kind nurse helped me recover. I also enjoyed horse racing there and even owned a horse that I raised from a pony.

I left Japan during a time of great change, specifically in the 31st year of the era that began in 1868, which is 1898. This was a time of modernization and increased overseas migration. I was about 17 years old when I decided to leave for the Pacific islands and later the mainland United States. That era shaped much of my early life and the opportunities I sought abroad.

When I first arrived in the Pacific islands and was waiting to go to a small island, I caught a serious bacterial infection. I was very sick with high fever and dysentery, and many others were dying around me. Fortunately, a kind nurse cared for me by feeding me cooked rice liquid, and I recovered. That illness was a major hardship but also marked a turning point as my ear troubles healed afterward.

I settled in a small city in California because I had relatives there, and I worked on my sister-in-law’s brother’s strawberry farm. Later, I operated my own farm growing strawberries on 20 to 21 acres. That town was home for me and my family for many years, where I raised my children and rebuilt after setbacks like the Great Depression and World War II.

I remember standing at a train station in California to see off my sister-in-law’s brother who was returning to serve in the military during the war with Russia. I held a flag of our homeland and waved it as the train left. It was a poignant moment reflecting the ties we still had to our country despite living in America.

After the war broke out, we were sent to a temporary assembly center in California as part of the forced evacuation of Japanese Americans. It was crowded and not very nice, but we tried not to grieve because so many were in the same situation. The food was adequate, but the toilets were terrible and left a lasting bad impression. We lined up for meals and lived with many inconveniences there.

Though my parents followed an Eastern religion, I was exposed to Christianity from a young age because my aunt married into one of the first Christian families in Japan. She often took me to church, and I learned about Christian beliefs through her and my uncle. This early exposure gave me a unique perspective on faith, even though I did not become Christian myself.

The Christian church my aunt belonged to was a small congregation affiliated with a Protestant denomination known for its emphasis on believer's baptism, one of the first in our area with only five or six believers. It was a humble congregation, but they were very conscientious about their faith. I remember how my aunt and uncle talked to me about Christianity within this denominational context during my childhood in Japan.

I attended middle school in Japan, completing about 12 years of schooling including elementary and junior high. The education was very formal and militaristic, with strict discipline and respect demanded for teachers and upperclassmen. The principal was loyal to the military, supported by an Army lieutenant colonel, and encouraged students to enter the military academy. I especially liked mathematics but was not fond of English, which was ironic since I later came to America.

I landed in a port city in Washington state at the end of 1906 after a 19-day sea voyage during which I was seasick for most of the trip. This city was my first stop in America, where I stayed one night before moving on to a larger city nearby. I was struck by the lush green trees growing along the seashore and the size of the town and roads.

From 1911 to 1915, I managed my own worker camp in a town near San Bernardino. I rented land, built houses, and gathered workers to send to orange groves and packing houses as needed. I ran the camp as a Christian house, prohibiting drinking, gambling, and other bad behaviors, and kept food costs low to benefit the workers.

The military junta governing the country was responsible for collaborating with the armed Karen group in attacking Mawker refugee camp in 1998. Their forces used mortars and grenades, causing destruction and fear among the refugees. This event was a pivotal moment in my family's journey to safety.
