Before flying to the United States, my family stayed in the capital city of Thailand for three days to complete physical check-ups and immunizations. It was the first time we were in a big city and the first time any of us had been on a plane. This city was a brief but important stop on our journey to safety.

Our final stop on the journey was the international airport serving the Twin Cities, where we were met by my father's family. It was the place where our new life in the United States truly began. From there, we went to stay at a local Baptist church before settling into an apartment.

I was born in a small East African country and grew up there surrounded by African culture. Even though I lived in Africa, I was familiar with American culture and dreamed of coming to the United States. That country is where my roots are, and it shaped who I am before I immigrated in 1996.

That iconic American quiz show was a big part of my childhood memories, especially watching it on my lolo’s lap in Illinois. It was one of the ways my grandparents immersed themselves in American culture and improved their English. I even remember pronouncing the show's name with a Filipino accent before I learned the correct English pronunciation in school.

The language my lolo sometimes used, especially when he swore, which could be quite startling to me as a child, connected me to my Filipino roots. It was part of the bilingual environment I grew up in. Though I was born in the U.S., hearing this language at home helped me maintain a link to my family's heritage.

I was born in a refugee camp in a Southeast Asian country after my family fled there following the communist takeover of Laos. This nation was a temporary refuge for my Hmong people, who escaped political turmoil in our homeland. It was in this place that my family held onto crucial immigration documents, like our I-94 cards, which allowed us to enter the United States later. Though we lived in difficult conditions, this country was the place that marked the beginning of our journey towards a new life.

After the war, my family and I fled to a neighboring Southeast Asian country in 1985 to escape the violence in Laos. We lived there for several years before finally coming to the United States in 1993. That country was a place of refuge but also hardship as we waited for a chance to start a new life. It marked an important chapter in my journey from war survivor to immigrant.

After fleeing my homeland, we crossed the river into a neighboring country, hoping for safety. We first stayed near the riverbank and later moved to places like Nong Khai and the Ban Vinai refugee camp. That country became a temporary home for us during those difficult years before we eventually emigrated to the United States in 1988.

My name is Yeng, born in 1947 in Moos Manj. I lived through the communist takeover, fled across the river with my family, and endured the loss of one of my babies during that escape. After years in refugee camps in the neighboring country, I emigrated to the United States in 1988, where I have raised my family and kept the memory of my lost child close to my heart.

A province in Thailand is where the refugee camp was located, and it became our temporary home starting in 1993. We lived there as refugees for over a decade, waiting for the chance to start a new life. That place holds memories of both hardship and hope for my family.

Before the war that began in 1914, I worked as a steward on a passenger steamship, which allowed me to travel to many places, including the vast continent east of Europe and south of Russia. This was part of my effort to see the world after my military service ended in 1912. Traveling there was an eye-opening experience that broadened my horizons before I eventually settled in the United States.

While working on the passenger steamship, I also had the opportunity to visit the continent south of North America. This was during the years between my army service and the outbreak of the war in 1914. Seeing that part of the world was part of my journey to explore before settling down in the U.S.

In the small town in the neighboring state where I raised my family, there were many immigrants from various countries, including the Scandinavian kingdom known for its forests and lakes. Though we mostly kept to ourselves at first and spoke only our native language at home, families from that northern European country were part of the diverse community that made the town a true melting pot. It was interesting to live among so many different cultures.

In 2012, this Midwestern state was the first to defeat a constitutional amendment that aimed to ban same-sex marriage. Aaron and I witnessed this historic event together, and it was a pivotal moment for us as a couple. It gave us hope and momentum leading up to our own marriage the following year.

gold_entity: Supreme Court of the United States
top_10: Supreme Court of the United States
Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution
Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution
implicit_text: The highest court in the United States played a crucial role in our lives when it overturned that federal law defining marriage. This decision allowed Aaron and me to marry legally in 2013, which was a deeply meaningful moment for us. It felt like a turning point not just personally but for many couples across the country.

I was born and lived in the capital city of my home country for 40 years. Most of my work family also came from there, and despite its pollution and traffic, it shaped who I am. Leaving that city was hard, but I wanted a better life for my children. I often miss my sister and my mom who still live there.

On our first visit to the country north of my homeland, we had the opportunity to visit some cousins who live in a coastal city in Southern California. It was a meaningful part of our trip, allowing my children to connect with family. This visit, along with stops in another large Southern California city and theme parks, made the experience unforgettable.

I was born in a historic city in Madhya Pradesh, India in 1998. Although my family moved to California before I was born, I came into this world there because my parents wanted me to be born in India. It holds a special place in my story as the starting point of my life before I moved back and forth between India and the US.

Traditional Indian classical music was another important part of my family's cultural environment growing up. Alongside popular film music, it filled our house and helped me stay connected to my Indian roots. These musical traditions were a constant presence during my childhood in both India and the US.

I knew the national anthem of the country where I was born from a young age, which was part of my connection to that nation despite living in the US. Learning it alongside the American Pledge of Allegiance reflected my bicultural identity. It symbolized the duality of my upbringing between India and America.

The country where my family lived in refugee camps for many years was in Southeast Asia. I was born in a refugee camp there in 1999, and later we moved to another camp in 2002 after a storm destroyed our home. Life in that country was difficult; my father struggled to find work and faced tough choices. Eventually, in 2011, we left that nation to start a new life in the United States.

Before moving to the United States, my family went to a border town in western Thailand for medical exams including X-rays and shots. During this time, the X-rays showed that my parents had tuberculosis, so they had to stay there for two more weeks for treatment. That town was an important step in the relocation process, ensuring we were healthy before traveling to America.

I was born in Klo Pa, a small town in Southeast Asia, in 1995, but there was very little there, so my family decided to move to a better place for our future. We moved to a refugee camp located in the same country, where I went to school and lived with my family. This country hosted the camp between Burma and itself, and it became my home until we moved to the United States in 2014.

The refugee camp in northern Thailand was where my family and I lived after leaving Klo Pa. I attended a high school there, which was located in section 13 of the camp. The camp was a place where many Karen people like me lived, and it became my home until we moved to St. Paul, Minnesota in 2014.

The country bordering the refugee camp where I lived is located next to northern Thailand. My school taught subjects including the languages of that country and Karen, reflecting our cultural ties there. Although I lived in Thailand, my identity as a Karen is deeply connected to that neighboring country, and this influenced my education and community life.

My great-grandfather was born in Belgium but lived in that southwestern German city, where he was a prominent figure in the Jewish community. He composed music for his synagogue, and those compositions are still used worldwide today. His legacy lives on through both his music and his family.

My family has a rich heritage of the religion centered on the Torah that has shaped our identity for generations. From my great-grandfather’s role in the Jewish community of the southwestern German city to my uncles becoming Judaic scholars and rabbis, this faith is central to our story. Despite hardships like Kristallnacht, our faith and culture have endured.

The rise of the fascist party in Germany marked a dark turning point for my family. Their ascent led to the destruction of the synagogue in that southwestern German city and forced my great-grandparents to flee for their lives in 1938. This party’s actions shattered the peace my family had known.
My grandfather’s brother served in the U.S. Navy and is pictured in his uniform after returning from combat in the Philippines. His military service is part of our family’s proud American story.

My great-uncle served in the U.S. Navy during World War II, including combat in the Philippines. His service is a proud part of our family’s American legacy. The Navy was a key part of his life before he returned home.

In 1959, my grandfather founded his realty company in that West Coast city. This company was the culmination of his years of education, military service, and business experience. Through this company, he became a successful industrial real estate agent, broker, and property developer. It was the business he built that allowed him to settle and thrive professionally.

I was born and raised in a Southeast Asian country in 1987, but my life there was very difficult because my father was a well-known Karen activist and peacemaker. I had to hide my true identity and live under a different name to avoid being captured or tortured by the military regime. After graduating with a social work degree, the military discovered who I really was, and I had to escape to a neighboring country to save my life.

After fleeing my home country, I lived in a refugee camp in a neighboring Southeast Asian nation for several years. My father sent men to escort me safely across the border, and I was introduced to a Karen doctor providing free healthcare to refugees along the border between my homeland and this country. I volunteered as a midwife, nurse educator, and counselor at her clinic while preparing to resettle in North America.

After fleeing Laos due to that secret conflict, my family lived in refugee camps in a neighboring Southeast Asian country before being granted entry into the United States. This country was a difficult but necessary stop on their journey to safety and a new life. It was in these camps that my parents endured hardship and uncertainty while hoping for a better future for their family.

The metropolitan area that includes the cities where I grew up is known for its vibrant Hmong community. This area became home to many Hmong families like mine after resettling in the United States. Growing up there shaped my identity and allowed me to stay connected to my culture while living in America.

This northern European country is where my great-grandmother was born and raised. She lived in a small town and was educated at a well-known university before she emigrated in 1902. She carried with her many items from her homeland, including gifts from her schoolchildren and personal belongings, as she knew she might never return. Her journey from this country to America marked the beginning of our family's immigrant story.

I was born in the capital city of Eritrea in 1988 and moved to the northern U.S. state known for its lakes in 2011. Since arriving here, I have been a student at a community college in the state's capital city. My family and I brought with us the jebena, a traditional Eritrean coffee maker, which holds a special place in my heart because it connects me to my homeland and family traditions. Through coffee ceremonies, I keep alive the memories of my childhood and the celebrations we had back home.

This city is where I was born in 1988, the capital of a country in the Horn of Africa. Growing up there, I experienced many family gatherings and cultural traditions, especially during holidays like Easter. It’s a place that shaped my early life and where my family and I shared many joyful moments, especially around coffee ceremonies. Even now, living in Minnesota, I often think back to that city and the warmth of those times.

This small country borders my home nation in the Horn of Africa. Like Eritrea, it is part of the region where the jebena coffee tradition is common. Knowing about this country helps me appreciate the cultural landscape of the Horn of Africa and the connections between our peoples.

A university in Kingston, Ontario played a pivotal role in Emil's life after he was granted a scholarship there, allowing him to complete his undergraduate and medical degrees. It was also where Emil met his wife, Eleanor Quinn. The university became a foundation for our family's education and future in North America.

A leading beverage corporation was the company that offered my grandfather a job, prompting our family's move to Atlanta, Georgia in the 1960s. At that time, this company was one of the city's major international corporations, providing employees with many benefits like access to clubs, private schools, and financing. Working for this corporation made it easier for my grandparents to settle and thrive in Atlanta.

During the 19th-century conflict, Canadians generally sided with the Northern forces, but in Atlanta, our family was perceived as separate from the North. This distinction helped my grandparents assimilate into Southern society without the stigma of being 'Yankees.' The Northern side's role in the war indirectly affected how our family was accepted in the South decades later.

I was born in the largest city of South Vietnam right at the fall of the southern regime in 1975. My family fled the city at the end of the American war in Vietnam, leaving with only a few hours' notice to get on a boat. This city is where my early life and memories of war began, shaping my identity as a Vietnamese-American activist.

I went to a refugee camp in the northern West Bank four months after the massacre in 2002 to see the aftermath of violence. Witnessing the destruction and hearing the stories of survivors was heartbreaking but necessary. It deepened my commitment to speaking out against injustice and war.

I was born in Mai Lah Ooo in 2000 after my family fled there as refugees. We lived in that Southeast Asian country for about a year before moving to the United States. It became a temporary safe place for my family and many other Karen people who were escaping violence in their homeland. Although life was hard, with little food and basic shelter, the weather there was mild and it gave us a chance to rest and rebuild.

Before coming to that Southeast Asian country, my family and the Karen people lived in a neighboring nation where we faced severe persecution from soldiers loyal to the ruling regime. They wanted us out, so we had to run for our lives while our villages were burned down. I remember hearing gunshots, screams, and smelling smoke as we fled. The journey from that country to the one next door was long and dangerous, with many people getting injured or worse. This neighboring nation is where our struggle began, and many Karen people are still running from the violence there.

I was born in the capital city of South Korea in 1966 and was told I was found abandoned there just days after birth. Later, I lived in an orphanage located in the northern part of that city before being adopted. Although I initially believed my birth story was true, I later discovered some of the information about that city and my early days there might have been fabricated.

I was placed in an orphanage located in the northern district of the capital city after living in the southeastern city, as I was considered 'adoptable' there. This orphanage is the only confirmed place from my early life before adoption. I even have a photograph of a friend from that orphanage, and I have been trying to reconnect with her ever since.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country in 1994, where many Hmong people lived after leaving Laos. My family stayed there for some time before we moved to the United States in 2004. That country was a place where several Hmong families, including mine, found refuge and continued their traditions.

After leaving sales, I partnered with my father-in-law to establish a cigar and tobacco company in the nation's capital. It became the city's biggest cigar distributor at the time, marking a major success in my business career. Despite personal losses, like the death of my first wife Sadie, I continued to grow the company and support my family.

top_10: Jewish Community Council of Greater Washington
implicit_text: I founded a community center that grew into a national organization promoting Jewish culture and education. This was part of my renewed dedication to my faith and community service after my remarriage to Yetta. The center stands as a testament to my commitment to supporting and enriching Jewish life in America.

My son made a significant donation to a museum dedicated to remembering the Holocaust, demonstrating our family's dedication to remembering and educating about that tragedy. This act continues the legacy of our commitment to Jewish history and community leadership. The museum plays a vital role in preserving the memory of those lost and educating future generations.

I was born on July 3rd, 1938, in Dundalk where my father was the chief of police. After my father passed away, I decided to enlist in the US Army and eventually left my homeland to serve overseas. That country will always be my homeland, but my journey took me far from there, shaping my life in ways I never imagined.

After the Philippines, I was stationed on an island chain in the Pacific for a few months. I kept requesting to be transferred to California because I wanted to be near Julia, but the military initially kept me there. That place was a temporary stop on my journey before I finally moved to New Jersey.

top_10: English as a second or foreign language
implicit_text: The form of English taught in schools in my home country alongside the national language was British English, and it influenced how we learned to speak and write. Growing up, I was exposed to British English norms in education, but in social settings, the local English variant was more common among my friends. British English serves as a foundation, but the local English variant has evolved uniquely from it, just as American English did from British English. This connection helped me understand the variations and legitimacy of different English dialects.

After leaving the Horn of Africa because of the unrest, I moved to a wealthy Gulf country and lived there for a few years. Although I couldn't continue my formal education in that Middle Eastern nation, I kept my curiosity alive and learned a lot about the country and its surroundings. My time there was marked by a thirst for knowledge, even without access to school.

The metropolitan area that includes Minneapolis and St. Paul has been my home since I came to the US. I often use an online search engine to understand the differences between places like Hennepin County and Ramsey County, or the two main cities themselves. This region has been central to my learning and adapting to life in America.

In the northern U.S. state, I became fascinated with the history of the Norse explorers, which I often researched using an online search engine. Learning about these seafaring people is part of my broader interest in the history of the area, including Native American stories. They represent an important cultural and historical topic that I explored after moving to the Twin Cities.

My husband was a member of the U.S. military branch known for its rigorous training and presence in Okinawa when we met. His role brought us together during a time of tension between Okinawans and American soldiers. Our marriage was a significant decision that changed my life path, leading me to move to North America.

The Pacific island state was our first stop when we arrived in North America in January 1966. After landing there, we stayed a few days before continuing on to Minnesota. It was a brief but important transition point from my old life in Okinawa to a completely new life in America.

I was born in Sofia, the capital of a Balkan country, where my family lived before moving to the United States. My mother worked as a tour guide at the National History Museum there before pursuing further education abroad. Leaving that country was difficult because we had to abandon a steady life, but the opportunities in the U.S. for my father's music career were too important to pass up.

I was born and raised in a Southeast Asian country, where I spent my early years surrounded by my family and friends. Life there was simple but meaningful, especially in my hometown where my parents worked making tofu and soy milk to sell. I studied up to 9th grade in that country before deciding to leave for better opportunities. Although I moved away, I still deeply miss my friends and the familiar places back home.

My family and I lived in a peaceful district within the largest city of my home country. It was a calm and quiet place compared to the busy city center, which made it a comfortable home for us. My parents ran their tofu and soy milk business there, and I spent my childhood in this neighborhood. Leaving that district was hard because it was where all my close friends and neighbors were.

I attended a high school in my home country, known as the 'Green School' because it was surrounded by many trees and everyone cared about protecting the environment. It was during my time there that I became addicted to video games, often spending whole days playing and even neglecting school. This school was an important part of my early education before I graduated 9th grade and moved to the United States.

The Southeast Asian country where my father comes from is part of my family's cultural background. Although I grew up in New York City, my parents' experiences in that country and Taiwan shaped how they raised me and my brother. The language and traditions they passed down helped me maintain a connection to that country despite living far away.

To escape the violence in my home country, my husband and I fled west into a neighboring kingdom known for its bustling cities and rich culture, where we lived in refugee camps. It was a difficult time, but being in that country gave us hope for a safer future. While in the camps, I dreamed of resettling in the United States, away from the constant bombings and gunfire we had endured.

After living in the first suburb, we moved to another suburb northeast of the Twin Cities, where my parents continued to run their restaurant. We stayed there until 2008, when the recession hit and we lost the business. This suburb became our home during those years, and my sister graduated from the local school district. It’s still where my family lives and tries to figure out new opportunities.

I worked in a Midwestern state for five years after completing my medical training, which was an important chapter in my career. This state provided me with valuable experience in family practice, and it was a place where I balanced work and family life. Despite the time I spent there, I eventually chose to return to Minnesota because of the welcoming community and the environment that suited my family best.

My homeland is a Southeast Asian country also known as Myanmar, specifically from a southeastern state inhabited by the Karen people. My grandfather taught me important lessons about time and calendars there, which helped me learn to read and write. Life in that country influenced my understanding of time management, which I carried with me when I moved abroad.

After crossing the large river, my family and I entered the neighboring Southeast Asian country, where we walked through the jungle for five days without food or shelter. We lived hidden in its jungles for about three years to stay safe. Later, around 1977, its government placed us in a refugee camp. This country was both a refuge and a place of hardship for us during those years.

My grandfather comes from the lineage of my great grandfather who was twice president, being one-eighth black. Despite our family’s past of enslavement, his bloodline rose to political significance and wealth in our West African homeland. His place in our family history connects me to both the struggles and successes of our ancestors. Through him, I understand the resilience and pride that define my family’s story.

My family moved to the capital of Puerto Rico in 1966 when I was two years old. I grew up there, attended school, and even college in that city. It was my home for over twenty years before I moved to the continental United States in 1987.

I arrived in a city in western Iowa in August 1987, looking for better opportunities in the United States. I lived there for nine years, working first in a meat-packing plant for four years, then four years at a computer manufacturing company known for its cow-spotted boxes. That city was where I started building my life in the US and where my daughter Tiffany was born in 1989.

I was born in the largest city of southern Vietnam, which was my home before the war changed everything. This city was the capital of the southern part of the country where I grew up and later served as an officer in the navy of that government. The city holds many memories for me, especially before 1975 when the government surrendered. Leaving that city was heartbreaking because it meant leaving my country behind.

The American naval forces played a crucial role in my escape from Vietnam. After fleeing the southern part of the country by boat in 1975, we were rescued by the U.S. naval fleet in the sea between Vietnam and the Philippines. Their intervention saved my life and the lives of many others, giving us a chance to start anew in the United States.

Currently, I work for the largest affordable housing developer in Minnesota, Iowa, and Wisconsin. As an advantage service specialist, I connect residents with resources and volunteers to help them achieve their goals. This role aligns with my long-standing interest in affordable housing and community support.

The city where I was born and where my family settled in 1997 after moving from Maryland is Houston, Texas. It’s the place where I grew up and where my parents worked hard to provide for me and my siblings. Our family was happy there until my father was deported in 2004, which changed everything. This city holds many memories of my early childhood and the resilience of my family during difficult times.

The county in Maryland where my parents met in 1989 after immigrating to the United States is where they got married and started our family, having my twin siblings in 1996 before moving to Houston in 1997. This place was where our family began and where my parents built their early life together in America.

I was born and raised in a Southeast Asian country where life was very hard for my family because we belong to an ethnic minority group. When I was about five years old, the national military restricted our movements and made it difficult to get enough food. My family lived in constant fear, especially after an incident when soldiers confronted my mother and me. Eventually, in 2006, my family decided to leave that country to escape the violence and hardship.

After leaving my home country in 2006, my family and I lived in a refugee camp in a neighboring Southeast Asian country for several years. Life there was difficult; we received free rice and food but had little money for other needs. My father worked hard, sneaking out to find jobs with local people, even though it was illegal, while my mother weaved traditional clothes from our ethnic group to help support us. We stayed there until 2012, when we were accepted for resettlement in the United States.

I was born in a country in Southeast Asia in 1999, a place surrounded by beautiful forests, rivers, and wildlife. We lived by hunting and farming, enjoying the sun and nature around us. However, the region was dangerous because of the civil war between the Karen people and the national soldiers. When I was just over a year old, our village was attacked by government soldiers who destroyed our homes and farms, forcing my family to flee for our lives.

After our village in the neighboring country was attacked, my family and I fled through the forest and eventually found safety in a nearby Southeast Asian nation. We were helped by soldiers from that country who guided us to a safer place far from the attacking forces. This nation became a refuge for us after escaping the violence. It was a place where we could finally feel safe, even though the journey there was very difficult.

This town in the neighboring Southeast Asian country is where my family and I finally arrived after fleeing from the conflict zone. It was a safe haven after the dangerous journey through the forest and the attacks by soldiers from our homeland. This place became our new home where we could live without fear of being attacked. It marked the end of a terrifying escape and the beginning of a new chapter for us.

I moved to the largest city in the United States in 1981 to attend a state university on a soccer scholarship. Arriving at the main international airport there, I was confident in my English but quickly realized the pace of American speech was challenging. That city became my home where I made friends, played soccer, and eventually started my family with three children.

While living in Germany, I watched the 1980 Winter Olympics, which left a strong impression on me. I was especially drawn to the U.S. team during the famous hockey game against the Soviet Union. This event shaped my positive views of America even before I moved there.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country and spent my early years there, growing up in a city rich with history and tradition. That country is where my roots are, and it shaped who I am deeply. Leaving in 2009 was difficult because I had to say goodbye to the culture I knew and loved. Even after moving, I carried my native culture and traditions with me wherever I went.

When I was a junior in high school, I went on a mission trip to a famous Louisiana city with my youth group. It was there that a memorable encounter happened—a lady greeted me with 'Aloha!' which surprised me because I had never met her before. That moment stayed with me and inspired me to write my poem 'My Name is Hmong: Call Me Freedom.' That city was the backdrop for a turning point in how I viewed my identity and culture.

After fleeing my homeland in 1979, my family and I crossed into a neighboring country where we lived in refugee camps. I worked as a nurse and public health worker helping both refugees and local people. This country was a place of refuge but also hardship, as we had to adapt to camp life and limited resources while hoping for resettlement.

After the first camp closed in 1992, my family was transferred to another camp in the same country. It was there that I registered and interviewed for resettlement to the United States. This camp was a transitional place where I prepared for a new life abroad and worked to bring my family to America for better opportunities.

I was born and lived in the country in Southeast Asia, where I served in the southern army from 1957 until the fall of its capital in 1975. That nation was the place where I fought and later suffered under the new communist regime. After the war ended, life there became very difficult for me and my family, especially during my time in the reeducation camp and the years of surveillance that followed.

I was born in a refugee camp in Southeast Asia in 1999. Life there was very hard and full of fear. We had to wait a month to get food, and even then, we had to pay with our own money, which was scarce. The refugee camp felt like a prison because we couldn't go anywhere freely without risking punishment.

During our journey to North America, we stopped in a border town in western Thailand to rest. The place was crowded and the bathrooms smelled bad, which was uncomfortable. Near our hotel, there was tall grass and a big round lake by the gate. While there, people from an international migration agency taught us how to live in America.

Albert Hanosh was one of Fahima’s older brothers who emigrated before her and helped establish the family in the United States. He worked as a grocer, a trade his father secured for him after arriving from Syria. Albert was part of the family’s early efforts to settle in Michigan by 1921.

Jamilia Hanosh, also known as Jimmy, is my grandmother and the daughter of Fahima Hanosh and John Oirathy. She was born and raised in Michigan, part of a family that valued assimilation and education. Jimmy married in 1950 and continued the family legacy into the next generation.

This newspaper published announcements about divorces during the Prohibition era. It was through this paper that I learned about Fahima Hanosh’s divorce from Joseph Anton, where she publicly accused him of making his own moonshine. This detail gives insight into the difficulties Fahima faced during that time.

This was a significant event during the 1920s that affected my great-grandmother Fahima Hanosh’s life. Her first husband, Joseph Anton, was accused of making his own moonshine, which was a serious offense then. This period was marked by strict alcohol laws and contributed to the difficulties in their marriage, ending in divorce in 1927.

I was born in a small Sicilian town in 1888, and in 1910 I married a man named Nicola and moved to Indianapolis. I was skilled with a needle and brought a trousseau filled with bedding and clothing as a symbol of my heritage and skills. Together with Nicola, we sold produce in Indianapolis and raised four children before I sadly passed away from tuberculosis in 1921 at the age of thirty-three. My legacy lives on through the items I left behind, now preserved and displayed to honor our Italian immigrant journey.

My husband was an Italian emigrant who lived in Indianapolis and returned to Sicily to find me as his wife in 1910. He was a produce vendor, selling fruits and vegetables from a horse-drawn cart in Indianapolis. Together, we built a life in the city, raising four children and working hard to support our family. His decision to bring me to America shaped the course of our lives and the future of our descendants in Indianapolis.

He was born around 1695 in Scotland and was a rebel soldier in the Jacobite Rebellion. After the failed uprising in 1715, he was captured and exiled to the American Colonies, where he served as an indentured servant for seven years. He eventually settled in Hanover County, Virginia, married around 1725, and had three sons who all remained in the area. He died in 1756 at about 60 years old, and his story is remarkable to me because he was about my age when he fought against the English and then rebuilt his life in Virginia.

He was the Protestant king supported by England during the early 1700s, opposing the Catholic James VII of Scotland. His forces defeated the rebels at Preston in 1715, effectively ending the uprising. This victory ensured his reign and led to the exile of many rebels like my ancestor to the American Colonies.

This county in Virginia became the new home for my ancestor after his exile from Scotland. He was likely indentured to a local landowner there and eventually married and raised his family in this county. It represents the place where he rebuilt his life and where his descendants lived for generations.

When my family arrived in North America in August 1978, we landed at a major airport in Minnesota. It was a big moment for us because I was only 12 and had never seen anything like the technology and conveniences there. My cousin had to teach us how to use things like toilets, electric ovens, microwaves, and showers, which were completely new to us. That airport was our first step into a very different life in America.

This neighboring country became our refuge after we fled Laos in 1975. We crossed the Mekong River at night to reach safety there, and my family was among the first to arrive at a refugee camp near the border. Life in that camp was very hard at first—there were no shelters, no electricity, and no running water. We built bamboo shelters and tried to make a home in the camp, where we stayed for three years before moving to North America.

After World War II, my sons Robert and Paul became involved in a scrap metal business that had ties to organized crime within the Italian community. They would steal aluminum shipments from the large aluminum company in New Jersey and resell the metal, leading to frequent government raids. Despite the risks, they had a network that warned them in advance, allowing them to hide the aluminum and continue their operations for some time.

My great Uncle Paul was a devout Catholic who frequently visited the religious center in Rome. He had the honor of singing for the pope during his visits, a testament to our family's strong Catholic faith. When he returned to New Jersey, he would sing sacred songs like 'Ave Maria' for our family, keeping our religious and cultural heritage alive.

'Ave Maria' is a song my great Uncle Paul often sang for our family after returning from the Vatican. It symbolizes our family's deep Catholic roots and connection to our Italian heritage. These songs remind us of the faith and traditions that sustained us through our challenging journey in America.

My grandmother was the eldest daughter of German farmers and spoke German until it was banned during the First World War. She was intelligent and tall, and she worked as a nurse manager in the state mental hospitals of Minnesota. She inspired my mother and was a key figure in our family’s history, embodying resilience through times of anti-immigrant sentiment.

This island nation is central to my family’s history through my grandfather, who left during the aftermath of the Irish Civil War. The conflict and its consequences, including his involvement with the Black and Tans, influenced his journey to America and our family’s story. Its history is a thread that connects me to a larger narrative of struggle and migration.

My father was involved in the civil rights movement when he moved from the southeastern U.S. to Minnesota. His commitment to social justice shaped my childhood and encouraged me to pursue academic excellence and sports. The civil rights movement was a significant influence on my family and my own identity.

My stepfather married my mother after her divorce. He is a Lutheran minister from a suburb of Minneapolis, with Scottish and Irish roots. He is a genuine person with a good sense of humor and a heart of gold, and he has been part of my evolving family story.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country in 1995, but life there was very difficult. Soldiers from the central government destroyed my house and farm, and my family had to live in temporary villages because of the ongoing battles between the Karen people and the national army. Our people were forced off our land and couldn't own property, making life unfair and dangerous.

In 1997, my family fled to a neighboring country to escape the violence in my homeland and lived in a refugee camp there. We stayed in that camp for more than twelve years, relying on support from international organizations for food, shelter, and clothing. It was a difficult but necessary refuge before we could start a new life elsewhere.

The coalition forces played a crucial role in liberating France when I was a child. I vividly remember the day when American soldiers arrived in our town as part of the coalition armies. Their presence brought relief and kindness, and my family even hosted some of the American GIs in our home. Their victory marked a turning point in my life and in the history of France.

The American army unit led by the famous general came through my hometown in northeastern France during the early 1940s conflict. I was just a child then, but I remember the excitement and hope their arrival brought. My family hosted some of the soldiers, and they shared food and kindness with us. That experience was my first real encounter with Americans and left a lasting impression on me.

I was born in the Southeast Asian country ruled by a military government, but we had to leave because my husband had problems with the authorities. The regime was unfair and cared only for themselves, not the people. Leaving that country was difficult, but it was necessary for our safety.

We stayed in the Southeast Asian kingdom at the refugee camp after fleeing my home country. This nation was a temporary place for us, but life was challenging and uncertain. It was there that I experienced many hardships before we were resettled to the United States.

gold_entity: Ronald M. Hubbs Center for Lifelong Learning
top_10: Ronald M. Hubbs Center for Lifelong Learning
implicit_text: I study English at a lifelong learning center in the capital city of Minnesota. Going to this center every day helps me improve my language skills despite my health issues and worries. It is an important part of my life here as I try to build a better future for my family.

I was born in a historic city in northern Syria in 1996, and it holds a special place in my heart as my birthplace and the home of my family. My father left that city in 1967 to seek opportunities in the United States, and years later, in 2000, my mother and I left the same city to join him. Even though I moved at a young age, I have vivid memories of that place and cherish the times I spent there during summers, which helped me understand the struggles my loved ones face. That city is more than just a place to me; it represents my roots, my culture, and the home I long to return to one day.

In 2013, I studied abroad in the Southeast Asian country known for its rich culture and history, which was a pivotal experience for me. While there, I learned about the increased racism toward Hmong communities, which made me feel guilty for being away during such a critical time. This motivated me to become more active in political and social justice movements once I returned to Minnesota.

Attending the major party’s national convention as a delegate for a progressive senator was a transformative moment in my life. It happened in the summer following the 2015 caucus, and it gave me the confidence to imagine running for office someday. This event solidified my commitment to breaking down oppression and being a voice for marginalized communities.

My great-great-grandfather was the patriarch who immigrated from central Europe with his family in 1882. He settled with us in Whitefish Bay, Wisconsin, and worked alongside his son weaving willow baskets to sell locally. His hard work and craftsmanship laid the foundation for our family's entrepreneurial spirit.

My son Emil was born in Milwaukee in 1887 and carried on the family tradition by opening our family furniture company in 1920. He built the business into a huge success, starting at 1020 North Broadway and later expanding to Plankinton Avenue. Emil’s dedication and skill helped establish our family’s reputation in the furniture industry.

The family furniture company was founded by my father Emil in 1920 in Milwaukee, starting at 1020 North Broadway. It grew to occupy six floors on Plankinton Avenue and was a family-run business passed down to my grandfather James. Although it closed in 1979, its legacy lives on, even featured in the Milwaukee Public Museum’s exhibit The Streets of Old Milwaukee.

My wife and I lived in the capital city of Germany for several years, where we had our first two daughters. I loved working there as an architect, which was my profession before moving to the United States. That city was an important chapter in both my family life and career.

I founded my bakery in the major northern city in May 2014 after losing my architecture job. It started from baking bread for my family and grew into a bakery offering over fifteen kinds of bread and German soft pretzels. The bakery has become a place where people come for authentic German bread, pastries, and pretzels, and it’s been rewarding to bring a slice of Germany to the community.

This traditional German festival is a big part of our business, especially because we sell almost a thousand German soft pretzels every week to local breweries around the metro area to go with beer. It reminds me of the traditional celebrations where pretzels and beer are staples. This event helps connect our bakery to German culture here in Minnesota.

The country where that southeastern region is located is where I was born. Life there was challenging for my family, which eventually led us to flee and become refugees. The political and social conditions in that country influenced our decision to leave and seek safety elsewhere. It will always be part of my identity as my birthplace.

This was the country where my family took refuge after leaving Burma. We lived in that refugee camp there, and my dad tried to find work outside the camp but faced difficulties, including being jailed for a month. We traveled through places like Mae Sot and Bangkok during our journey to the United States. It was both a place of refuge and struggle for us.

This town in Thailand was one of the places we traveled through on our way from the refugee camp to the United States. It was part of the long and worrying journey my family took in 2011. I remember being scared and seeing my younger brother faint in the car because of the smell on the dirt road there. This town was a difficult but important stop on our path to a new life.

In the capital city of Thailand, we went through airport security on our way to the United States. I didn't understand why the security took away our belongings, and I thought they might arrive in the U.S. before us. The airport experience was confusing and stressful for me as a child. This city was a key point in our journey to America.

The official language of the Himalayan kingdom became a requirement for citizenship under the 1980 laws imposed by the government there. My parents and grandparents, who are of Nepali origin, did not speak this language and instead had their own languages and customs. This language requirement was used as a tool of exclusion and persecution against our community. It was one of the reasons my family was forced to flee that kingdom and live as refugees in a neighboring country.

Our journey to the United States began with a brief stay in a major Florida city, where we stayed at a hotel for one day after arriving on December 8th, 2016. This city was our first stop before moving on to our final destination in Minnesota’s capital. It was a mix of excitement and fear as I traveled by plane for the first time and faced a new environment. This city marked the beginning of our new life in America.

In February 1868, my son and I sailed from the Pacific coast city on a steamship named America to Panama as part of our return journey to Canada. From Panama, we then boarded the Dakota to continue to New York. The America was a key vessel in our trip back after our difficult time on the west coast.

Thailand was a temporary refuge for my family and me after fleeing my home country. We lived in a refugee camp there, which was a difficult but necessary place for survival. This country provided the space where we waited and hoped for a new life, eventually leading to our move to the United States.

My name is Rodrigo Botero Ibarra, born in Cali, Colombia in 1970. I studied topographic engineering at a major public university in Valle del Cauca and worked as a topographer and university instructor. Later, I pursued a master's degree at a well-known private university in Bogotá and am currently applying for a PhD in mathematics at a large research university in the United States. I moved to the northern Midwestern state in 2014 with my family to continue my studies and be close to my parents and siblings. My goal is to advance my academic career and eventually return to my home country to work in research.

My father's biological father came from a small town in central Italy to the United States in 1909. He settled in a steel town near Lake Erie in New York. He married my grandmother in late 1916, and they had three children, including my father. Sadly, he died in November 1918 from the Spanish flu at the Italian hospital in Buffalo.

This town in New York was where my paternal family settled after arriving from Italy. Located on the shores of a Great Lake, it was home to the largest steel plant in the world by 1900. My grandparents lived there, and my father was born there. It was also where my family later placed markers on the graves of relatives who died during the 1918 flu in the local cemetery.

This region in southern Italy is where my maternal grandmother was from. Specifically, she was born near a city called Cosenza in a small town. It represents my mother's roots before she and her family emigrated to the United States in 1927.

This northern province of the southern Italian region is near where my maternal grandmother was born. The small town of her birth lies close to this city. This area is central to my mother's family history before they moved to the United States.

This cemetery in the steel town of New York is where several members of my paternal family who died from the 1918 flu were buried. The graves were originally unmarked, so my father, siblings, and I pooled money to place markers there. This cemetery holds a lot of family history and memories for us.

This Southeast Asian kingdom played a significant role in my life, especially during the time I attended university in its capital city in 1960. Later, after fleeing my homeland in 1975, my family stayed in a refugee camp in Ubon, located in the northeastern region of this country, for several months before moving to the United States. This nation was a place of both education and refuge for me and my family during difficult times.

After fleeing my homeland in 1975, my family and I lived in a refugee camp located in the northeastern province of a Southeast Asian country for about 7 or 8 months. It was there that we were interviewed and eventually sponsored to resettle in the United States. The camp was a temporary but crucial stop on our journey to a new life, and it was also where we met Dr. Jerry, who helped us find a home in Rochester, Minnesota.

After living in Rochester for many years, my family moved to the metropolitan area encompassing Minneapolis and Saint Paul. This move allowed us to be closer to a larger Lao community where I serve as a Mor Phone, officiating ceremonies and supporting cultural traditions. This metropolitan area has become an important place for continuing my community work and preserving our heritage.

In 1845, his family moved from their farm in the Highlands to a major industrial city to work in factories and save money for passage to the New World. That city was a tough industrial place that contrasted sharply with their rural life, but it was a necessary step for the family’s migration. This move set the stage for their eventual voyage to Canada and the challenges that followed.

My home country was where I lived until 2013 when my family and I moved to the United States. Life there was good; I attended one of the top schools and had a supportive social circle. Leaving that country meant leaving behind a familiar culture and language, which was very challenging when I arrived in the US.

One week after arriving in the US, I enrolled in 11th grade at the public high school in Richfield to prepare for a college entrance exam. It was difficult to blend in because of my limited English skills, and I disliked group work and discussions since I had to speak a lot. Despite these challenges, attending that high school was an important step in adjusting to life in the United States.

That country was not my home, but I lived there in the refugee camp near the border. After fleeing Burma, my family stayed there where we received new clothes and stayed in a hotel before flying to the United States. Although it was a tiny community and life was hard, I remember feeling fresh and hopeful when we prepared to leave. The journey through that place was difficult, but it was a necessary step before starting our new life abroad.

My family originally came from a Southeast Asian country, but we had to flee because we feared the government there. We left everything behind to find safety and peace. I was very young and don’t remember much about what I left, but I do remember the last time I played with my friends before we escaped. That country was the place we called home, but it was no longer safe for us to stay.

I am currently the director of a nonprofit organization’s elder center in a major northern city. This organization allows me to support elders in my community and stay connected to my cultural roots. It is rewarding to give back and help others in this role.

After fleeing my homeland and crossing a major river in 1984, my family and I lived in a neighboring Southeast Asian country as refugees until 1987. That country was a temporary home where we waited before immigrating to North America. It was during this time that I replaced my broken flute and prepared for a new life.

During the covert conflict, my father fought against communist forces from a neighboring country in Southeast Asia. This conflict deeply affected my family and led to my father's death. Those forces were the opposing side in the war that shaped my early life and eventual migration.

After high school, I wanted to study economics but my math grades weren't sufficient, so I chose nursing at a leading university in Ghana. I completed a four-year nursing program there, passed the nursing exam, and started working in hospitals. That university was where I gained my foundational nursing education that prepared me for my career both in Ghana and later in the United States.

I was born in a small town in the Pescara province of Italy, on July 23, 1925. It was a peaceful place where I had a wonderful childhood with my family. However, life became difficult there when the Fascist government took control, and my family decided to leave for a better future.

My husband was the son of Scandinavian immigrants, so that Northern European country was part of his heritage. Our marriage in the early 1950s united my Italian background with his roots from that region. Though I was deeply connected to Italy, I embraced the cultural blend that our family represented in Illinois.

During World War II, while studying in a major Midwestern city, I worked as a hostess at a wartime service organization. It was an exciting and freeing experience that connected me with many new friends and the wartime community. This role was very different from my traditional upbringing and helped me embrace my new life in America.

Two of my brothers served in the American army during World War II, which was a proud and worrying time for our family. Their service represented our commitment to our new country, even as we maintained our Italian heritage. Their sacrifices during the war were deeply respected by all of us.

I spent several years doing missionary work in a Southeast Asian kingdom after settling in the United States. This experience allowed me to connect with different cultures and give back in meaningful ways. That country was a place where I could extend my faith and service beyond my own story, enriching my life and broadening my perspective. My time there was an important chapter in my journey as an immigrant and a missionary.

I was born in Klo Pa in 1995, but my family didn't stay there permanently. In 2003, we moved from that country to a refugee camp near its border. That place was a point of transition for us as we sought safety from the violence in our homeland.

I was born in a country torn by ongoing civil war between ethnic rebel groups and the military regime. Soldiers often raided our village, burning houses, killing people, and harming women, which made us live in constant fear. My parents decided to move us away to find peace and safety, which eventually led us to a neighboring country and later to the United States.

I was raised in a South American country, but I left in my mid-twenties due to political instability, economic difficulties, and concerns for my personal safety. Life there was dangerous; I remember being at a party with 17 people and 12 handguns on the table, including mine. I knew I had to leave to find a better future, which led me to the United States.

One of the two publications under my media company that I oversee provides valuable resources to the Hispanic community, helping them navigate education, healthcare, and other systems. This magazine is part of my effort to give back and support immigrants like myself.

The independence organization for the island was something my grandmother got involved with after witnessing injustices like the blood tax during the war in Southeast Asia. She attended many of their protests, even when they turned dangerous, because she believed strongly in fighting for the island's independence from the mainland. Joining this group was part of her activism and passion for justice during her younger years on the island.

My grandmother worked at a child welfare agency in Charleston for thirty years as both a case worker and an interpreter. Her passion for fighting injustice and helping those who couldn't always fight for themselves drove her commitment to this work. Even with decades of service, she often faced intolerance due to her accent and background, like being asked for her green card at the DMV. Her dedication to the community is a big part of her story here in the mainland.

After leaving the country to the north, my mother and family crossed a major river to the neighboring kingdom in 1975, where they stayed in a refugee camp. My father reunited with them there before we emigrated to the United States in 1976. That kingdom was a crucial refuge for the Hmong people during those turbulent times after the fall of Saigon.

In 2007, I traveled to a wealthy Gulf country and stayed there for four years before moving to the United States. My time in this Middle Eastern nation was a significant transition period away from my homeland and my family. It was an important step in my journey before settling in America.

One of the traditional dishes I always remember from my childhood in East Africa is a spiced raw beef dish. My family would gather together to eat this along with other dishes during celebrations and family times. It’s a dish that brings back warm memories of home and togetherness.

My mother comes from a town in a coastal province of the Philippines. While I was growing up there, my mom worked hard to support our family, often sending photos and letters to my dad in the U.S. Army. This town is where my mother’s side of the family is from, and it’s part of the background that shaped my early years. Even though I moved to the United States later, it remains a meaningful place in my family history.

We first visited a city in Ontario in 2003 during a vacation, where some of my parents' close university friends lived. My parents loved how safe and friendly this city felt compared to the UK, especially given the rising crime rates there at the time. This visit was pivotal because it convinced them that this place was the right location to raise me and my brother. It was the starting point of our family's immigration journey to Canada. That city holds a special place in our story as the place that inspired our move.

I was born in a refugee camp in Thailand in 1999. My parents and sister fled Burma in 1997 because of the civil war and settled in this camp. Life there was hard; we didn’t have freedom to leave or find work, and food was scarce. Although I had a chance to go to school, educational opportunities were very limited. This difficult life in that camp is why my family decided to move to the United States in 2012.

This Southeast Asian country was where I was born and where my family lived in a refugee camp after fleeing Burma in 1997. Even though we were safe from the war, life there was restrictive; we couldn’t leave the camp or work freely, and food was scarce. I went to school there but didn’t have many chances to learn. In 2012, my family decided to leave this country and move to the United States for a better life. Leaving was emotional, especially saying goodbye to my cousin and friends.

My parents and sister were born in the country now known as Myanmar, and all my relatives come from there. Because of the civil war in that country, my family had to leave in 1997 and seek safety in a refugee camp in Thailand. The conflict there shaped my family’s journey and the hardships we faced as refugees. Even though I was born in Thailand, my roots and family history are deeply connected to that country. The war there was the reason we eventually moved to the United States in 2012.

When we arrived at the main airport serving the Twin Cities in Minnesota, my cousin and some friends were waiting to pick us up. Seeing familiar faces after a long journey made me feel both joyful and weary. It was a warm welcome that helped me feel more at home in this new place. This airport marked the end of our long travel from Thailand and the start of our life in the United States. It was a very important moment for my family and me.

I was born in a large city in southwestern Nigeria in 1998. Growing up there, I had a good childhood despite the country being considered a third-world nation. My family lived there until I was ten, when we decided to move to the United States after winning the Diversity Visa Lottery.

I continued my education by obtaining a Master of Arts in Education from a public university located in Indiana. This advanced degree helped me become a better teacher and support my students, especially refugees and youth from Indochina. It was an important step in my professional development.

After fleeing my homeland, my family and I crossed into a neighboring Southeast Asian country, which was a critical refuge before we could come to North America. It was there, in that country, that we stayed briefly while making arrangements to resettle far away. This place represents a key step in our long journey from war to safety.

I have worked closely with my ethnic community, especially in Minnesota, helping to set up a Parent Teacher Association to support youth and parents in understanding the American education system. Many of the students I taught felt like family to me, and I was committed to helping them succeed. Supporting this community has been a vital part of my life and work.

My mom was born and raised in the metropolitan area of Minnesota’s two largest cities, so that’s where her roots are. Even though I was born in Los Angeles, my family moved to Minnesota when I was a baby, and I’ve lived here ever since. That metro area represents my mom’s background and the place where my family settled after leaving California. It’s a big part of my identity growing up as a second-generation Mexican immigrant.

My father joined the naval forces of the United States when he was 19 after barely passing the English aptitude test. He was stationed in Yokosuka, Japan, where he met my mother. His service in the military was pivotal, as it not only shaped his career but also led to my birth on the military base in 1993. After finishing his term, he used his degree earned during service to start a career in St. Petersburg, Florida, where we moved as a family.

I was born in a Japanese city hosting a U.S. naval base in 1993 and lived there until I was six years old. During those years, I was surrounded by my mother’s family, and my childhood memories are deeply tied to the Japanese language and culture. That city was the place where my multicultural identity began to form, but moving away from there to the United States was difficult because it meant leaving behind the culture I considered central to who I was.

I was born in a Southeast Asian country in 1999, and even though I left when I was very young, that country was always a part of my school lessons in the refugee camp in Thailand. I remember my geography teacher gave us homework about that place, and we had to memorize a long paragraph about it. It was a subject that connected me to my roots while I was living far away in the camp.

When I was two years old, my family moved to a neighboring Southeast Asian country, where I grew up in a refugee camp and went to school. The school was very basic, made of bamboo, leaves, and wood, and it was often damaged by rain and wind. Life there was challenging, but I was excited to go to school every day, even though the teachers were strict and the punishments harsh. I sang the Karen National Anthem every morning and wore a uniform, and my experiences there shaped much of my early life.

The refugee camp where we lived was located in a Southeast Asian country near the border with Burma. My family stayed in camps in this country for many years after fleeing Burma. It was there that we faced the flash flood in 2002 that destroyed our camp and forced us to move to another camp nearby. This country was the place where my early childhood was shaped by the refugee experience.

My parents originally came from a Southeast Asian country, which is why we had to flee and live in refugee camps along the border in Thailand. That country is the one we left behind due to difficult circumstances, and many of our relatives still live there. Some family members chose to stay because they were afraid of starting a new life elsewhere or had to care for elderly parents. It is the homeland that shaped my family's history and the reason for our refugee journey.

This major metropolitan area is close to where I grew up, in Seongnam. Although I didn't live directly in this bustling capital, its proximity was part of my early life experience in my home country. My move from this region marked the beginning of my international journey to Japan and later the United States.

After leaving the Black Sea port, we traveled to a Scottish city known for its shipyards before boarding the Furnessia to America. This city was a stopover on our difficult journey from Russia to the immigration station in New York Harbor. It was part of the route that brought us closer to our new life in the United States.

My family lived in the western borderlands of the Russian Empire, an area where Jewish people were confined and faced many hardships. The Pogroms and anti-Jewish attacks there made life dangerous, so my parents decided to leave for America. That restricted zone was the place we escaped from in search of freedom.

When we first arrived in America, my family only spoke the language of Eastern European Jews, which made adjusting to life here challenging. It took time to learn English and adapt to the new culture. That language was the language of our home and community during those early years.

I was determined to get an education despite my family's financial struggles. After dropping out to help support my family, I convinced my parents to let me return to school. I graduated from a high school named after a prominent educator, which was a proud moment for me.

The violent anti-Jewish attacks in the Russian Empire made life unbearable for my family. These attacks in the restricted western territories were a major reason my parents decided to leave that region. Escaping those attacks meant we could finally live without fear in America.

The ruling party of China invaded the Himalayan region in 1985, forcing me and many others to flee our homeland. Their occupation took away the freedom of the local people and made life unbearable. Because of their actions, I had to escape secretly, leaving behind my family and childhood to seek safety.

The island state of Hawaii was where my mother and I first set foot in North America in 1975. Many immigrants from my birth country entered through this state after the 1965 immigration law, so it was a familiar gateway for us. From there, we moved on to the mainland to find better job opportunities.

After arriving in Hawaii, my mother and I moved to the capital of New York State in June 1975. My mother was searching for a well-paying job, and we hoped to find prosperity there. I started eighth grade in a predominantly white middle school, where I faced a lot of racial and cultural challenges.

A historic neighborhood in Los Angeles is where my mother and I found a vibrant community of people from my birth country after moving there in 1977. This neighborhood gave us a chance to immerse ourselves in our culture through food, language, and social events. It was a turning point for me in reclaiming my cultural identity.

After my ex-husband retired, we moved to a western state known for its mountains to start a new chapter in our lives. It was here that my friend Ceci told me about an English class, which I decided to take despite the challenges. The teacher and other students were very patient and helpful, and I enjoyed the class. Living in this state has been a positive experience as I continue to maintain good relationships with my family and friends.

My home country is an archipelagic nation in Southeast Asia where I was born and raised, specifically in its bustling capital. I completed my high school education there before moving to the U.S. in 1998. Growing up in that country, English was part of the national curriculum, which helped me prepare for studying abroad.

I started my higher education at a community college in the Midwestern state, the same school my sister attended. I spent two years there taking basic and liberal arts classes to ease into studying in English. This experience helped me transition smoothly before transferring to the large public university in the same state.

The first American city where my great-great-great-grandfather John Francis Coad Sr and his family settled after arriving from Ireland was the capital of New York state. According to his 1910 obituary, the family lived there before moving westward. This city represents the initial foothold my family had in the United States during the 1850s.

My family helped found a major Jesuit university in Nebraska, which is part of the legacy left by my ancestors. This involvement in education highlights the broader impact the Coad family had beyond business and politics. The Jesuit university stands as a testament to their commitment to community and learning.

Growing up in the Southeast Asian archipelago, specifically its bustling capital, was a place full of energy and warmth during my childhood. Living there was a sharp contrast to other places I lived, with its loud, friendly streets and open community. It was a formative experience that showed me a different way of life, full of movement and connection.

The northern European capital gave me quiet and space to think during my time there. It taught me that silence doesn’t mean disinterest and that deep relationships can grow slowly and steadily, like a winter thaw. This experience helped me appreciate a different pace of life and the value of patience in building connections.

The historic capital of a central European country was a city where I found a sense of independence and curiosity. Walking its streets alone, I discovered a place steeped in history that welcomed me to be myself without explanation. It was a meaningful chapter in my journey of identity and belonging.

This southern Italian town was where my grandparents lived, perched on a hill overlooking a dry river valley. They lived in ancient cave dwellings that were overcrowded and unsanitary. The poverty there was so severe it was famously described in a memoir by an Italian author. This town was the origin of my family before they moved to another town and eventually emigrated to America.

This river runs through the valley below the town where my grandparents lived. Their home overlooked this dry valley, a harsh landscape that shaped their difficult lives in the cave dwellings. The river and valley were part of the environment of poverty and hardship that my family endured before emigrating.

These cave neighborhoods in the southern Italian town were where my grandparents lived. These dwellings were carved into the mountain and were overcrowded and primitive, with people and animals sharing the same space. The conditions were so dire that an Italian author described them vividly in his memoir. Growing up hearing about these caves made me aware of the harsh realities my family faced in Italy.

Today, the southern Italian town and its cave neighborhoods are recognized as a World Heritage Site, celebrated for their historical and cultural significance. This is a stark contrast to the desperate conditions my grandparents experienced there. While the site is now quiet and picturesque, for my family it was a heritage they sought to leave behind in search of a better life.

top_10: United States Department of the Army
implicit_text: I served in the military of this country for a while after leaving home as a teenager. This experience was part of my early adult life before I moved around the country and abroad. The military was a chapter that shaped my journey and added to my restless spirit.

This neighborhood in a New York City borough is where my mother's relatives, including her cousin Giulia, lived. Although Anna was from this borough, we chose to live apart from her family in this neighborhood and another.
