The city in southern California was where I took the train to boot camp and met other Marines like John O'Connor. It was the gateway to our Marine training in California. The journey there was filled with anticipation and the start of our transformation into Marines.

The hotel in Milwaukee where my friends and I stayed the night before processing into the military had the entire eighth floor reserved for people joining various service branches, and it turned into a big party. I remember a woman who was going into the Army and was quite drunk, trying to sleep with as many guys as possible, which eventually got us kicked out of the hotel.

Another potential boot camp location I asked about during my processing was a city in Southern California, but I was told there was a meningitis outbreak there at the time. I had never heard of these outbreaks before, and it influenced my assignment to the Great Lakes training center instead. That city was part of the options but ultimately not where I went for training.

I joined the state militia on October 6, 1940, and was inducted into federal service on October 15, 1940. Before that, I had some monthly training, but this was my first full-time service. The militia was my entry into military life just before the U.S. entered World War II.

Our first military training camp was a facility in Louisiana named after a Confederate general. I remember arriving there in the rain and helping cook bacon and eggs that ended up soaked with water. We had to set up our own tents and trained there before Pearl Harbor, using older equipment like 30 caliber machine guns.

I sailed on a U.S. Navy cruiser from San Francisco to Australia in April 1942. It was a large ship carrying freight and troops, but not heavily armed, so we were vulnerable if we met enemy battleships. The journey took about three weeks to reach Adelaide.

We went to a camp near a major Queensland city before heading to New Guinea. I was on the docks there and thought the convoy was lost because it was all woods around. That city was a staging area for our further deployment into the jungle combat zones.

While wounded in New Guinea, I was first treated by a medic from a Midwestern state known for its Hoosier nickname. He was one of the first to attend to me before I was evacuated to hospitals in Australia. That state was represented in the medical personnel who helped me on the battlefield.

I was born in a city in southern Wisconsin near the Illinois border, but we moved when I was about four and a half years old. So, while that city is my birthplace, I really grew up elsewhere. It’s where my early life began before my family relocated.

My father worked for the local gas and electric utility in the capital city of Wisconsin, in the accounting department as a bill collector. During the Depression, it was tough on our family, but he kept his job there. That utility company was part of our family’s livelihood while I was growing up in that city.

One of the big stars who came to perform at the theater where I worked was a renowned actress admired for her talent. She admired my writing and even invited me to join the theater guild, which was a tempting offer but would have meant moving to New York. It was quite an honor to be recognized by someone of her stature during those days.

The air force of the neighboring country was where my friend served during the global conflict. He made it overseas and survived the war. I had considered joining their forces myself early on, but ultimately decided to enlist in the U.S. Air Corps instead.

I participated in the Reserve Officers' Training Corps while attending the major public university in the capital city of Wisconsin, which helped me when I enlisted in the Air Corps cadet program. The military indoctrination I received there gave me a foundation that made the transition to officer training smoother. It was a valuable experience before the war.

My grandfather fought in the Civil War and was wounded at a famous early battle. Despite being on opposite sides, he and a veteran from the other side became good friends later in life. That family history gave me a perspective on camaraderie and reconciliation that I carried into my own military service.

A city in Alabama was where a training field was located, and I went there for basic training after primary flight school. Unfortunately, I never got off the ground there and was eventually washed out. It was a tough experience but part of my overall military journey.
The transcript does not provide specific details about my experience with a particular military transport aircraft, so I cannot speak about it from my own story.

I grew up in a small community in Wisconsin after being born in that Iowa river city. It’s near where I went to school and where my family still lives. I worked at a supper club there during my high school years, bussing tables on weekends.

I started my college education at a university in a southwestern Wisconsin city for two years before transferring. It was close to where I was serving in the National Guard, so it made sense to start there. This was part of my undergraduate path before moving on to a university in northern Iowa.

My dad served in a well-known airborne infantry division, although he never deployed to combat. His service influenced my understanding of military life, even if he was more casual about it. The airborne tradition runs strong in my family.

After 9/11, I was called back on temporary duty to a military installation in Virginia to assist with street security as part of a military police unit. Although I was in an audible role, I helped with driving and other support tasks. That base was where I re-engaged with military service during the heightened security period.

A historic fort in Minneapolis was where I drilled with the Army Reserve unit while attending law school. It was a convenient location that helped me maintain my military obligations during that time. That fort was part of my military journey before returning to active duty.

When I re-enlisted in 2003 after being called up with my engineer battalion, I went through the military entrance processing station again like a new recruit. The adjutant general expedited my processing because of the urgent mobilization. That station was where I officially re-entered active military service after years in the Individual Ready Reserve and Reserve.

I was born in the capital city of what is now the Czech Republic. It was there in the 1930s and during World War II that my early life was shaped. My grandfather was a Unitarian minister in that city and suffered greatly under the Gestapo during the German occupation. Those years in that historic city left a deep impression on me and influenced my decision to move to the United States after the war.

During the German occupation of the central European capital, the secret police was a constant threat to my family. They suspected my grandfather, a Unitarian minister, because he listened to the BBC and spoke hopeful words about the future. One morning, they raided his home and arrested him, leading to his death in a concentration camp. The secret police also took my aunt to a forced labor camp, making those years very dangerous for us.

Listening to the British Broadcasting Corporation on shortwave radio was illegal and punishable by death during the German occupation of the central European capital. My family had a shortwave radio and a card to prove we were allowed to listen, but it was still very risky. My grandfather was caught listening to that station early one morning, which led to his arrest by the secret police. That broadcaster was a vital source of news and hope for us during those dark times.

My basic training took place at a military base in Arkansas, where I learned artillery skills with the 105 millimeter howitzer. It was there that I also took language tests and leadership courses, hoping to use my language skills in the army. That base was the starting point of my military service before being sent overseas to Japan and then Korea.

While in East Asia preparing for Korea, I saw local soldiers dismantle and clean M1 Garand rifles with impressive skill. They knew more about those rifles than I did after basic training. We tested the rifles at a range before moving on to Korea. The M1 Garand was a key weapon during my military service.

top_10: United States Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps
implicit_text: I enlisted in the U.S. naval forces in July 1944, just before turning eighteen. I initially wanted to serve on submarines but was assigned to the amphibious group instead. I trained at Great Lakes, Fort Pierce, and Newport before serving aboard a newly commissioned attack transport during World War II and was later called back during the Korean War.

My grandfather served in the Austrian military, and I have a picture of him in uniform. This was part of my family's European military heritage, which included both Austrian and German service. Though I never met him, his service was a point of pride in our family history.

After basic training at the naval station near Chicago, my amphibious group went to a Florida coastal base in October 1944 for two months of specialized amphibious training. We practiced landings with landing craft and did night maneuvers to prepare for potential beach assaults. The barracks there were screened but open-sided due to the warm climate.

During my induction process, I was placed temporarily with inmates from a reformatory in Green Bay. At that time, most inmates were white, unlike today. This experience was unusual and memorable as I was processed alongside these men before entering the Army.

Another Navy pilot training course was offered at my high school during my senior year as part of the military's effort to maintain educated men during the war. I passed the qualification tests but could not join due to my eyesight.

After the Army camp in Illinois, I was sent to a fort in Brooklyn, New York, where I joined an infantry regiment composed mostly of Italian-American soldiers. I was the only one without an Italian-sounding name. That fort was where I learned to handle weapons and experienced early Army life.

There was talk of a strike among the soldiers at the Brooklyn fort, and some were threatened with being sent to a penal colony located at a camp in New York. I was included in that group initially but was unaware of the details. This incident led to my transfer out of that unit.

I was sent to a university in Deland, Florida, as part of a college training program after leaving the Brooklyn fort. Unfortunately, the program was shut down shortly after I arrived. While there, I attended classes and found ways to sneak out and enjoy campus life.

The Army camp was located on land that had once been the farm of a man who later became a Congressman or Senator. His name was associated with the area, and we even trained on terrain called Pepper Hill, linking the camp to his legacy.

I was inducted into the Army at the Illinois military post near Lake Michigan in early December 1941, just days before the attack on the naval base in Hawaii. We stayed there for about a week, completing physicals and receiving our uniforms before moving on to Camp Barkeley, Texas. That post was the starting point of my military journey.

top_10: University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center
implicit_text: After the induction site in Illinois, I was sent to the Texas medical training center near Abilene, which was expanding rapidly. I completed my basic medical training there and stayed on as cadre for several training cycles. It was also where I first saw a notice recruiting for airborne troops, which changed my path.

By the time I was training for airborne, the division famous for its red patch was already fighting overseas in North Africa and Sicily. Their success was well known and inspired many of us to volunteer for airborne duty. This division set the standard for airborne operations during the war.

I mentioned the famous American author when talking about the era we grew up in, especially his book that captured the struggles of that time. His work resonated with many of us who lived through the economic hardship and drought. It helped frame the context of our youth before the war.

While I didn't train specifically in the Deep South, the Georgia military base where I did my parachute training is part of that region. The Deep South had many military installations that were crucial for training troops during the global conflict of the early 1940s. It was a significant area for preparing soldiers for combat.

The northern city was where I joined the 73rd Engineer Combat Battalion after arriving in Korea. It was a big change from home, with no stores and a very different environment. We had a houseboy who helped keep our living area clean, which was a small comfort in a tough place.

I remember firing a standard-issue semi-automatic rifle during training and patrols. The rifle had a strong kick, and the first time I fired it, I wasn't holding it right and got a cut under my eye. We also had to take these rifles apart and reassemble them quickly as part of our training at a port city in Korea.

We used a shoulder-fired anti-tank weapon in training and it was actually easier to fire than the standard rifle because it had no kick. We practiced firing at old jeeps and tanks, and it was a lot of fun. That weapon was a memorable part of my combat training experience.

After leaving that Wisconsin city, I went to the large metropolitan area known for its twin urban centers as part of my transition into the Army. It was a stopover before heading to Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, for basic training and AIT in early 1965. That metropolitan area was just a waypoint on my way from home to the Army life.

After Jump School, I was assigned to a cavalry division that had just been renamed from an air assault division. This was in mid-1965, and the 1st Brigade of the division was preparing to deploy to Southeast Asia. I left for Vietnam in August 1965 as part of this division, which was a big moment for me coming from Wisconsin to a combat zone.

The transport ship was the vessel that carried me and about three thousand soldiers of the 1st Brigade from Savannah, Georgia, to Vietnam in August 1965. The trip took 32 days and included surviving a hurricane and a twelve-hour stop in Hawaii. It was a long and challenging journey for a farm kid like me going into combat for the first time.

During our voyage to Vietnam in 1965 aboard the transport ship, we stopped in Hawaii for about twelve hours. That was my first real exposure to a place outside the Midwest, and it was a brief but memorable stop on the way to the war zone. Coming from Wisconsin, Hawaii was a big change and part of the long journey to Vietnam.

I decided to join the branch of the military known for its tough amphibious training in 1944 because I didn't want to be in the Army or Navy. I went through boot camp at the West Coast recruit depot in San Diego and experienced the tough training there. During my time, I was part of the 27th, 31st, and 34th training battalions on the West Coast. This branch shaped my early adult life and gave me a sense of purpose.

I didn't want to join the naval service, so I chose the amphibious infantry instead. However, I did encounter a shore patrol sailor while returning from furlough, who helped me with traveler's orders when I was three days late getting back. The naval shore patrol played a role in maintaining order among servicemen during that time.

Arnie Martin was a kid I used to play ball with, and we joined the tough amphibious infantry together in 1944. While I was sent to San Diego for boot camp, Arnie was sent to the East Coast base at Camp Lejeune and then to another recruit depot at Parris Island. We started this journey together, but our paths diverged based on the assignments within that branch.
